articles.title: Best practices and ethical considerations for crowd-sourced data in the behavioral sciences
articles.abstract: Many researchers in the psychological and behavioral sciences use crowd-sourcing platforms to collect data, and yet many of those researchers are poorly informed about the participant-experience on these platforms. In this paper we summarize our experience in participating in many hundreds of research tasks. We present a list of common ethical and practical short-comings perpetrated by researchers, as well as a corresponding list of recommendations for designing research that facilitates quality data, maintains ethical standards, and improves the quality-of-life of participants. We also identify how the validity of many common and standardized methods are threatened by the presence of non-naive survey-takers. Importantly, this paper includes considerable feedback and guidance from the microworker community. While there is good research being executed on crowd-sourcing platforms, we contend that most research on these platforms could be improved by adopting a subset of the recommendations herein.

articles.title: When retraction replaces rebuttal: suppression of critical scholarship on parental alienation
articles.abstract: Parental alienation remains one of the most contested concepts in psychiatry, psychology, law, and social work, having been excluded from the DSM-5 and removed from the ICD-11 while continuing to play a substantive role in custody proceedings internationally. This opinion article does not revisit the scientific case regarding parental alienation but instead documents a distinct and underexplored problem, namely the pattern in which proponents have responded to critical scholarship through organized campaigns to retract, withdraw, or remove that work from the record rather than through the ordinary mechanisms of scientific debate. A search of the published record failed to identify any instance in which critics have initiated comparable retraction campaigns against proponents, who have instead engaged through conventional academic channels. The asymmetry documented here aligns with established frameworks on the suppression of scientific dissent and parallels mechanisms previously identified in tobacco and climate research. The argument advanced does not depend on resolving the underlying validity question, because the integrity of scientific discourse requires that disagreement be addressed through evidence rather than through administrative mechanisms designed for a different purpose.

articles.title: Structural Monster: How Organizational Harm Amplifies Structural Awareness and Becomes Externalized
articles.abstract: This paper proposes the concept of the Structural Monster to explain why some individuals who experience organizational harm do not simply remain silent, withdraw, or become psychologically immobilized, but instead begin to analyze, record, conceptualize, theorize, and externalize the structures behind their experiences. The Structural Monster refers to a state in which an individual silenced by organizational harm comes to understand personal experience not merely as private memory, but as structure, and attempts to record, preserve, conceptualize, theorize, and externalize it. By connecting research on organizational silence, psychological safety, workplace bullying, abusive supervision, labeling, narrative construction, institutional defensiveness, documentary power, externalization media, and generative AI, this paper develops a theoretical model of how organizational harm can be transformed into externalized structural awareness. The central argument is that AI does not create the Structural Monster. Rather, Structural Monsters emerge from organizational harm, existential erasure, narrative fixation, and institutional defensiveness. Generative AI functions not as a cause, but as a contemporary externalization medium that helps organize, conceptualize, translate, and theorize structural awareness that has already emerged. The paper models Structural Monster formation as a process involving organizational harm, existential erasure, narrative fixation, hyper-structural awareness, structural preservation behavior, the use of externalization media, and the eventual emergence of the Structural Monster. It also situates the Structural Monster within a historical sequence of externalization media, including dialogue, writing, paper, print, newspapers, the internet, social media, and generative AI. The theoretical contribution of this paper lies in redefining the victim not merely as a silent, damaged, or withdrawing subject, but as a figure who may preserve, externalize, and theorize structure. Practically, the paper argues that when organizations label problem-raisers as difficult individuals, silence them, and process them through formal procedures or sanctions, they may unintentionally intensify hyper-structural awareness and externalization. The paper concludes that contemporary organizations do not need techniques for silencing problem-raisers; rather, they need the capacity to engage in dialogue about the structures to which problem-raising points.

articles.title: Challenges to intergroup cultural transmission in humans
articles.authors: Patricio Cruz y Celis Peniche, Cristina Moya
articles.abstract: Despite its ubiquity, the spread of cultural traits across group boundaries is not easily reconciled with many cultural transmission biases that encourage stability rather than change. Adaptation to local environments and coordination costs should motivate parochial and conformist learning. It seems surprising that humans would ever benefit from copying outgroups, particularly when such models are rare, and copying them can be penalized by one’s ingroup. While we readily copy better traits or successful demonstrators, including from outgroup models, indicators of success are often group-specific and harder to observe in outgroups, reducing their perceived value. As such, it is unclear how learners evaluate outgroup traits while minimizing the risks of copying the wrong trait, copying the right trait wrongly, or being punished by their ingroup regardless. In this review we address how and why intergroup cultural transmission (or IGCT) happens despite its potential pitfalls, and how learners manage these risks. We propose a functional taxonomy of forces affecting IGCT that considers the role of non-normative versus normative costs, informational costs, and whether traits’ payoffs are frequency-dependent. We review formal models and experimental, ethnographic, and historical case studies that illustrate these dynamics, ending with a discussion of unanswered questions.

articles.title: From the integration paradox to migration aspirations: Return migration, onward migration, or immobility?
articles.abstract: Despite extensive research on the integration paradox, its behavioral consequences remain largely unexamined. Although integration is expected to foster long-term settlement, structurally integrated migrants often report heightened discrimination. We argue that these experiences increase aspirations to leave, but their translation into migration plans depends on migrants’ capabilities. Building on an aspirations–capabilities framework, we conceptualize education as a key determinant of migration capabilities distinguishing between absolute and relative education. Absolute education captures skill transferability across destinations, whereas relative education reflects migrants’ position within the origin-country distribution and associated status expectations. We further argue that return and onward migration follow distinct evaluative logics: return is shaped by relative status considerations vis-à-vis the origin country, while onward migration reflects perceived skill transferability in alternative destinations. Using data from the International Mobility Panel of Migrants (IMPa), a large-scale survey of immigrants in Germany (N=41,600), we show migrants with higher absolute education are more likely to respond to alienation by planning onward migration. Contrary to our expectations, higher relative education is also associated with increased onward migration, while those with lower relative education are more likely to consider return.

articles.title: The Temporal Turn in Social Movement Studies: Political Readings of Temporality
articles.abstract: The study of temporality has gained increasing prominence in sociology and social movement studies. As this wave of research on temporality has risen, scholars have increasingly argued that time should not be understood as a neutral backdrop but as a dimension of power and conflict. Building on the concept of the capitalist timescape as a socio‐political regime that organises, synchronises, and commodifies time through dynamics of acceleration and permanent crisis, this emerging critical field shows how temporal logics discipline everyday rhythms, compress horizons of political action, and foreclose alternative futures. Fighting against the capitalist temporal order is a battlefield of social movements which do not simply exist in time but act upon it, generating counter‐temporalities that reconfigure pasts, presents, and futures. By explicitly bridging the sociology of time and social movement studies, this article shows that the temporal turn cannot be understood without situating social movements within broader socio‐political regimes of temporality. Recent scholarship has articulated this “temporal turn” in Social Movement Studies, highlighting how social movements mobilise concepts such as critical junctures, memory, rhythm, anticipation, and prefiguration to contest the capitalist timescape. While contributions have multiplied in recent years, they remain fragmented across these different strands. This article provides an analytical overview of this literature and calls for a more integrated framework to capture how affective, symbolic, and practical practices of temporal agency intersect. It argues that grasping time politically is crucial to deconstruct the capitalist temporal order, and strengthen the strategies of resistance that emerge against it.

articles.title: Escape or Attack? Chinese “Nan niang” Subculture on Bilibili: Gender Performativity and the Cost of Belonging
articles.abstract: In China‘s official discourse, the promotion of “masculine vigor” has intensified in recent years. Yet, on Bilibili, a video platform anchored in ACG culture, the “Nan niang” phenomenon:biologically male individuals performing highly feminized identities,is flourishing. This contradiction reveals a strategic retreat among young Chinese men facing the collapse of hegemonic masculinity, mounting pressures in the marriage market, and post-pandemic economic precarity. Drawing on systematic observation of 12 “Nan niang” influencers and discourse analysis of approximately 20,000 user comments, this study reveals that the “Nan niang” phenomenon and the Western manosphere constitute mirror responses to the same social pressures: the former turns inward, performing femininity to dissolve masculine anxiety; the latter turns outward, attacking women to reclaim a hyper-masculine identity. Bilibili’s ACG culture and “moe” mechanism depoliticize and aestheticize gender transgression, providing a unique “gender comfort zone” for Nan niang practitioners. The paper proposes two localized concepts: the “cost of belonging” (the economic and psychological price men pay to be recognized as “qualified men”) and “escapism-through-community” (the strategy by which subcultural groups collectively evade real-world pressures), offering a new analytical framework for understanding marginalized groups’ digital survival strategies in a risk society.

articles.title: Epistemic injustice in scientometric analysis of African academic literature. A Foucauldian discourse analysis of limitation texts
articles.abstract: Scientometric analyses are widely used to study research, yet they often rely on global citation databases with uneven coverage of the certain countries and regions. This study examines how such limitations are addressed within the literature itself. Using Foucauldian Discourse Analysis, a corpus of scientometric studies on African research is analysed to explore how authors construct African science, justify methodological choices, and position themselves in relation to database exclusions. The analysis shows that African scholarship is frequently rendered partially invisible, framed as a hypothetical risk, or naturalized as an inevitable limitation of bibliometric data. At the same time, discourses of excellence and methodological authority are mobilized to sustain the validity of these studies while deferring responsibility for inclusivity to future research. These practices may reproduce forms of meta-insensitive or active ignorance, contributing to contributory epistemic injustice in representations of African science.

articles.title: Sequencing Peace: How Patterns of Cooperation Shape Public Opinion on Rapprochement
articles.abstract: How does the trajectory of cooperation from a foreign adversary shape public support for peace? Existing research conceptualizes cooperative signals observed through rapprochement as isolated events, yet citizens often assess an adversary's behavior retrospectively and use these temporal records to infer future intentions. We argue that these cooperation trajectories matter because, even when rapprochement consists of positive interactions overall, negative changes in cooperation can erode public evaluations by resonating with prior beliefs that the adversary is inherently hostile. Thus, citizens exposed to temporal patterns in which cooperation deteriorates or alternates with decline form lower expectations of future cooperation and express stronger resistance to peace initiatives. We test this argument using two survey experiments: a hypothetical U.S.–China rapprochement scenario in the South China Sea (N = 2,324) and a selective-history experiment examining the 2018 inter-Korean rapprochement in South Korea (N = 2,000). Across both studies, respondents exposed to inconsistent or descending cooperation trajectories anticipated lower levels of future adversarial cooperation and showed less support for conciliatory policies than those exposed to alternative trajectories or cooperation without a discernible pattern. These findings explain why rapprochement that features exchanges of meaningful concessions often fails to secure the public endorsement necessary for durable peace.

articles.title: External News Efficacy: Introducing an understudied mediator in news trust choices
articles.authors: Augusto Valeriani, Diego Garusi, Gaetano Scaduto
articles.abstract: Research shows that unmet journalistic expectations undermine trust, yet the cognitive mechanisms linking the two remain underexplored. As new journalistic actors and formats reshape journalist–audience relationships, this becomes an important area of inquiry. Drawing on external efficacy research, we introduce “external news efficacy”, defined as individuals’ perception that journalists are accessible, disclosive in their decision-making processes, caring, influenceable by, and receptive to ordinary people. We hypothesize that the construct mediates the relationship between journalistic expectation–evaluation discrepancies and trust in news media. Using a structural equation model on a representative sample of Italian adults, results show that such discrepancies reduce trust partly by lowering external news efficacy.

articles.title: Informal Rules as Regulatory Ecosystems: Integration, Disintegration, Drift, and Fragmentation in Regulatory Governance Systems
articles.abstract: Regulatory governance is formally designed through rules, mandates, and procedures, yet enacted through informal rule systems that shape how authority is interpreted in practice. This article develops the Informal Rules Ecosystem Model, explaining how informal norms, tacit routines, social expectations, and informal enforcement mechanisms interact to produce system level dynamics. The model accounts for integration, disintegration, drift, and fragmentation, showing why formally similar arrangements generate divergent execution time behaviour. Using a conceptual model building approach and a multi agency regulatory illustration, the article demonstrates how informal rule ecosystems shape information sharing, joint enforcement, and threshold interpretation across regulatory settings.

articles.title: Exploring Administrative Burden and Burnout Among Italian Oncologists: A Cross‑Sectional Survey
articles.authors: Davide Petruzzelli, Monica Giordano, Giuseppe Aprile, Alexia Bertuzzi, Luisa Fioretto, Alessandro Marra, Paola Varese, Davide Integlia
articles.abstract: Administrative burden has become an increasingly prominent organizational issue in healthcare, with potential consequences for clinical practice, quality of care, and physician well‑being. This study assessed the extent and perceived impact of administrative burden among Italian medical oncologists through a national cross‑sectional web‑based survey conducted between February and March 2025 among clinicians affiliated with the Italian College of Chiefs of Medical Oncology Units (CIPOMO). A total of 179 valid questionnaires were analysed. Oncologists reported dedicating an average of 41.0% of their working time to administrative activities, predominantly related to form completion, management of information‑technology system failures, and data entry or data management. Activities most frequently perceived as appropriate for delegation included information‑technology troubleshooting, inventory control, patient reception, and appointment scheduling. Nearly half of respondents reported experiencing burnout in the previous six months, with over one fifth identifying administrative workload as the primary contributing factor. These findings indicate that administrative burden represents a substantial component of oncologists’ daily work and is perceived as limiting time available for core clinical activities, while contributing to professional distress. Organizational strategies aimed at redistributing non‑clinical tasks and strengthening administrative support may help mitigate these effects and promote more sustainable oncology practice.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Dressed for the Festival, Made for the Landfill: Dark Patterns, Cultural Appropriation, and Greenwashing in Indian Fashion E-Commerce
articles.abstract: India's fashion e-commerce sector is among the world's fastest-growing, with the online fashion retail segment projected to expand at a compound annual growth rate of 24.2% between 2025 and 2032. Major Indian fashion platforms routinely deploy culturally resonant visual design, including festival imagery, regional craft motifs, handloom aesthetics, and artisan-adjacent language, as central drivers of seasonal consumption. This paper develops a conceptual framework for understanding how such design choices perform a double function: they accelerate fast fashion purchasing by anchoring it in cultural legitimacy, while simultaneously appropriating the sustainability signifiers of the craft traditions they visually invoke. Drawing on scholarship in human-computer interaction, design ethics, consumer behavior, and postcolonial cultural studies, as well as first-hand practice-based research in Indian craft documentation and festive retail design, this paper makes three connected theoretical claims. Indian festival retail interface design functions as a culturally amplified consumption accelerant, combining established dark-pattern mechanisms with emotional cultural legitimacy in ways that existing HCI literature has not examined together. Handloom and craft aesthetics are regularly extracted from their social and ecological contexts and redeployed as sustainability signals in campaigns that primarily sell industrially produced fast fashion. Sustainability programs appended to volume-consumption architectures are structurally incapable of addressing the harm that the core interface is designed to produce. The paper closes by proposing a research agenda for empirical work and directions for responsible design practice at the intersection of cultural heritage, e-commerce UX, and environmental accountability.

articles.title: A sustainability conceptual classification system to support socio-economic indicator design and evaluation in fisheries and aquaculture
articles.authors: Amber Himes-Cornell, Kristin R. Hoelting, Lucy Bowser, Chiara Buonfrate, Gwendal Le Fol, Xavier Nelson-Rowntree, Priscilla Rivas
articles.abstract: Social and economic sustainability indicators are widely used to inform fisheries and aquaculture management, yet there is no common framework for developing, selecting, evaluating, or comparing them. A shared framework would bring coherence to this landscape by supporting the systematic identification of gaps and opportunities to strengthen understanding of social and economic dimensions across both sectors. In response to this need, the United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) and the ICES Working Group on Social Indicators undertook a scoping literature review to better understand how social and economic indicators have been used in fisheries and aquaculture management. This article describes the development and iterative refinement of a conceptual classification system, grounded in established sustainability frameworks and refined through pilot testing, that FAO developed to guide the selection and use of social and economic sustainability indicators in these two sectors. The system organizes the diverse concepts represented by indicators in the fisheries and aquaculture literature into a coherent set of categories and sub-categories. It offers a practical tool for evaluating and comparing the conceptual comprehensiveness of existing data collection efforts and for supporting the robust design of indicators in new initiatives.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Crypto for the few? Social inequality and the embedding of cryptocurrency in wealth portfolios
articles.abstract: Cryptocurrencies have become an increasingly prominent component of household wealth portfolios. While existing research has primarily examined who owns cryptocurrencies, much less is known about how cryptocurrencies are embedded within broader portfolio structures and whether crypto investors constitute a distinct social group. Using an online survey of adults in Germany (N = 1,207), this study examines who invests in cryptocurrencies, how crypto is embedded within broader asset portfolios, and whether crypto investors form a homogenous or internally differentiated group. We combine logistic regression models with latent class and latent profile analyses. Logistic regressions identify socio-demographic, economic, attitudinal, and ideological correlates of cryptocurrency ownership. The latent class analysis examines how cryptocurrencies are embedded within broader ownership portfolios, while the latent profile analysis investigates heterogeneity in asset values among crypto-oriented investors. Cryptocurrency ownership is strongly stratified by age, gender, and risk tolerance, while associations with education and income become less pronounced once ideological orientations are considered. Portfolio analyses provide little evidence for a distinct crypto investor class. Instead, cryptocurrency ownership is embedded within broader structures of wealth accumulation and financial participation. Crypto investors are distributed across multiple portfolio profiles, including a financially oriented crypto-intensive group and a highly diversified asset elite that combines cryptocurrencies with conventional assets. Overall, the findings suggest that cryptocurrencies primarily extend rather than fundamentally transform existing patterns of financial inequality. By jointly analyzing ownership, portfolio composition, and asset values, the study contributes to sociological research on financialization, digital assets, and wealth inequality.

articles.title: Access Is Not Action: An Agent-Based Model of Climate Information Flow and Usability in Institutional Networks
articles.authors: Ivan Puga-Gonzalez, Marta Bruno Soares, Vanja Falck, Pierre Van Wolleghem, Larissa Lopes Lima, F. LeRon Shults
articles.abstract: This study examines the conditions under which climate information becomes usable within institutional networks. Although substantial efforts have been devoted to improving the production and dissemination of climate information, a persistent gap remains between information availability and its practical use in adaptation decision-making. Existing research has identified numerous barriers and enabling conditions affecting climate information use, but less attention has been given to how these factors interact within institutional networks to shape usability outcomes. To address this gap, we develop an empirically grounded agent-based model of climate information flow and usability based on the network of ICLEI Europe and its member municipalities. The model integrates three complementary empirical sources: survey data on information creation, request, sharing, and exchange; qualitative interviews examining how climate information is translated into institutional action; and secondary datasets used as proxy indicators for institutional, political, and contextual conditions. Together, these data inform both the structure of the network and the behavioural mechanisms governing information use. The model simulates the creation, exchange, and evaluation of climate information between municipalities and explores how institutional capacity, peer support, co-creation, and political barriers influence usability. Results show that information availability within the network is consistently high, with most information requests being fulfilled. However, information use remains comparatively low, revealing a persistent access–use gap. Sensitivity analyses indicate that usability is primarily shaped by accumulated experience, peer support, and political and institutional barriers. Strong peer networks and collaborative experience can substantially improve usability. Methodologically, the study demonstrates how survey data, interview findings, and proxy indicators can be systematically integrated into an agent-based modelling framework. Substantively, it highlights that improving adaptation outcomes requires greater attention to the conditions that enable information use, rather than focusing exclusively on increasing information provision.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Picking Winners or Marking Them? Timing-Based Evaluation of Innovation Certification
articles.authors: Angelo Leogrande, Mauro Di Molfetta, Valeria Nortarnicola, Maria Giovanna Trotta
articles.abstract: Voluntary certification policies — which let firms self-nominate for innovative or privileged status — increasingly channel fiscal, financial, and regulatory support, yet evaluating them is deceptively hard: certified firms may outperform because certification works, or simply because firms apply when already rising. We show that voluntary certification can certify winners precisely because rational firms time entry to moments of transitory expansion: the measured premium largely reflects when firms participate, not what participation does. We formalise this as anticipatory take-up: because the benefits are most valuable while a firm scales, forward-looking firms register at the crest of a growth run-up. The mechanism yields three predictions — a cross-sectional premium, a premium that largely predates certification, and entry driven by recent growth, not profitability. We test them in the Italian innovative-SME regime, linking panel data for roughly 2,900 certified and 1,200 non-certified SMEs to each firm's registration date and sector, and treating performance as a six-dimensional vector. Certified firms grow far faster than balanced peers yet are financially less solid, especially when smaller and capital-intensive. Once registration timing is exploited through event-study and staggered difference-in-differences designs, the premium proves largely selection: about 81 per cent of the revenue advantage predates registration, and the residual merely continues a pre-existing trend. A hazard model confirms it — entry rises with recent growth and smaller size, not profitability. The paper reframes the evaluation of voluntary certification from average effects to observable dynamic selection: such schemes mark firms already on distinctive trajectories rather than create them. The lesson generalises — robustness to omitted variables is not robustness to selection on trends, and cross-sectional evaluations mislead unless they exploit the timing of take-up.

articles.title: Social Heterogeneity and Technology Adoption in Colonial India
articles.abstract: Our paper examines whether local social heterogeneity influenced the adoption of ring-spinning technology in colonial Indian cotton mills during the interwar period. While existing literature emphasises factor prices, raw material constraints, and managerial choices, we highlight the role of social structure in shaping firm level technological outcomes. Using a mill level dataset coupled with census-based caste heterogeneity and religious composition, we analyse variation in the scale and intensity of ring-spinning adoption. We find that caste heterogeneity was negatively associated with the absolute numbers of ring spindle adoption. In contrast, religious composition does not significantly affect the scale of adoption but is positively associated with ring intensity which is share of ring in total spindles, indicating a distinct channel through which social structure shaped mill level technological configuration. These findings contribute to the economic history of technological diffusion by demonstrating that social heterogeneity influenced firms’ ability to organise labour and scale modern technologies, highlighting the importance of social structure for industrial development.

articles.title: A Computational Analysis of Millions of Scam and Spam Robocalls Targeting Older Adults and the General Public
articles.abstract: Robocalls that use autodialers or prerecorded messages remain a dominant channel for spam (commercial solicitations) and scams (attempts to steal money or information), yet their psychological characteristics at scale remain poorly understood. We leveraged a honeypot (decoy phone lines to receive and transcribe unsolicited calls) of 295,749 lines to analyze 4.49 million transcripts over five years (2018–2022). A large language model, validated against fraud expert judgments, classified calls as scam, spam, or neither, and as targeting older adults or the general public. Transcripts were profiled using LIWC-22, a fraud tactics dictionary (promise of gain, threat of loss, time pressure, authority), and emotional arousal indices. Scam robocalls showed higher arousal, time pressure, threats of loss, and authority appeals than spam. Scams targeting older adults showed even higher arousal, time pressure, loss-framing, and health content – patterns linked to scam susceptibility in older adults – with implications for theory and intervention design.

articles.title: Impossible Values in Canonical Cognitive Dissonance Studies and their Early Replications
articles.abstract: The GRIM test and other mathematical techniques for detecting impossible reported values in psychological studies have brought increased attention to the importance of reliability, replicability, and research integrity in psychology. The past decade has also seen historical research into canonical psychology studies showing that many famous studies were misreported, selectively reported, or potentially unreliable. In this article, I use the GRIM test and similar techniques to re-evaluate two canonical cognitive dissonance studies: Aronson and Mills (1959) which introduced the paradigm of effort justification and Festinger and Carlsmith (1959) which introduced the paradigm of induced compliance. Then I evaluate a key early replication of each study: Cohen’s replication of induced compliance and Gerard and Mathewson’s replication of the effort justification effect. I document impossible means, standard deviations, sums of squares, and sample sizes across all four studies.

articles.title: Arabic Translation, Cultural Adaptation and Psychometric Validation of the Historical Intergenerational Trauma Transmission Questionnaire (HITT-Q) in an Arab Sample
articles.authors: Jennifer Tarabay, Dennis Golm, Ellen Hedstrom, Nick Maguire
articles.abstract: Objectives: No culturally validated Arabic historical intergenerational trauma (IGT) measure exists despite widespread conflict and displacement in the Arab world. This study aimed to translate, culturally adapt, and psychometrically validate the Historical Intergenerational Trauma Transmission Questionnaire in an Arab sample (HITT-Q). Design: This study conducted: (1) cross-cultural translation and adaptation processes; (2) factor analyses, internal consistency and correlation tests to assess HITT-Q’s reliability and validity. Methods: Following forward–backward translation into Modern Standard Arabic, cultural adaptation was guided by a focus group. Twelve items were adapted and two were added. The final version was administered to a diverse Arab sample (N = 302) alongside validated Arabic translated mental health measures. Results: Exploratory factor analysis supported a four-factor structure, capturing Family Vulnerability, Intergenerational Resilience, Offspring Vulnerability, and Group Victimisation, and proposed an adapted HITT model. All four subscales demonstrated very good to excellent internal consistency (α = .87–.94). As expected, higher family vulnerability was linked to greater trauma symptoms (r = .47***), anxiety (r = .42***), depression (r = .50***), and group victimisation (r = .303***), and was negatively associated with resilience (r = –.39***). Family and offspring vulnerability were also significantly correlated (r = .39***), suggesting that risk may be transmitted across generations (***p < .001). Conclusions: The initial data support the reliability and construct validity of the Arabic HITT-Q. Therefore, it holds potential for clinical assessment, research, and interventions aimed at breaking cycles of IGT in affected families. This work contributes to Sustainable Developmental Goals such as Good Health and Well-being (SDG3). The Arabic HITT-Q has been included in the supplementary material of this article. Keywords Intergenerational trauma, cultural adaptation, psychometric validation, intergenerational resilience, Arab population, good health and well-being

articles.title: Stigma Dynamics
articles.abstract: How does stigma emerge and fade away? While previous research has examined how stigma can be experienced, managed, or capitalised upon, we have a limited understanding of how these processes fit into the more general dynamics of (de)stigmatisation. This chapter seeks to consolidate and deepen our comprehension of (de)stigmatisation processes and dynamics from the initial motivation of audiences, including the rationalisation of their tainted judgement, to how stigma can be normalised. The chapter also discusses some core conceptual pillars that explain stigmatisation processes such as categories and emotions. It then bridges the gap with other relevant literature that can offer new ways of understanding stigmatisation and destigmatisation, including micro- and macro-foundations and other broad theories in the field of organisation studies.

articles.title: Population mental health in England, 2003–2022: Teasing apart psychological distress, mental illness and activity limitations
articles.abstract: Background: Despite much public debate in England on mental ill-health, yet little distinction is made between different types of mental health measures. This matters because different measures may reflect different processes — rising distress, the labelling of distress as mental illness, or exclusion. We examine trends in all three among the working-age population (aged 16-64) over two decades. Methods: We analyse repeated cross-sectional data from the Health Survey for England (2003–2022; n=109,113). Psychological distress is measured using the GHQ-12 (caseness threshold ≥4); self-reported mental illness using questions on longstanding health conditions; and activity limitations using follow-up questions on how conditions affect day-to-day activities. Survey-weighted logistic regression models estimate trends overall, by age, and conditional on psychological distress. Findings: Psychological distress increased from 13.6% (95% CI 13.2–14.1%) in 2003–2006 to 19.1% (17.6–20.7%) in 2022, with the sharpest rise occurring before the COVID-19 pandemic. Self-reported longstanding mental illness rose more than threefold, from 3.7% (3.5–3.9%) to 12.2% (10.9–13.4%). Among people with high psychological distress, the proportion also self-identifying as having a longstanding mental illness rose substantially 2013–2014 to 2022, from 23.9% (20.6–27.5%) to 32.4% (28.1–37.2%). Mental health-related activity limitations increased more modestly: reports of being limited "a little" rose from 2.1% to 4.0%, whereas severe limitations remained stable. Interpretation: Different measures of mental ill-health have followed divergent trajectories in England. Psychological distress has risen, but self-reported mental illness has increased disproportionately to distress and unmatched by increases in activity limitations. This suggests that some form of medicalisation/exclusion is occurring alongside rising distress.

articles.title: An Amplifier of Position, Not Competence: Why Hierarchy Emerges, What We Pay for It to Hold, and Why Its Cost Cannot Be Settled
articles.abstract: A prior trilogy established that individual outcomes are dominated by the dispersion of the environment rather than of capability, formally Var(ln ρ_eff) ≫ Var(ln k) at a global dominance ratio R ∈ [27,134], but not where that dispersion comes from. We supply the mechanism: hierarchy as an amplifier, a multiplicative cascade that turns tiny, often capability-irrelevant initial differences into the large positional dispersion the trilogy measured, so that R becomes an output of structure rather than a fact about people. We microfound the feedback f(S) in Gould's deference model (linear at the bottom, saturating at the top) and identify the Kesten reset that creates the heavy tail with Boehm's reverse-dominance insurance, pinning the tail index in closed form, α = 1 + [δ + √(δ² + 2σ²p)]/σ², confirmed by a Clauset MLE. The calibrated amplifier reproduces R at all four aggregation levels and requires a near-random-walk between-environment process. On the raw ICIJ Offshore Leaks officer network the predicted heavy tail holds (global α ≈ 2.25), but no concentration metric tracks recording opacity across jurisdictions: that negative result vindicates the demarcation paper, since the quantity that would test the lever is hidden by construction. We then turn to a domain where position is logged, GitHub pull requests. On 113,895 closed pull requests, position multiplies the merge odds 3–7× at fixed diff size; a within-author design bounds the position premium at 12.6 pp (p = 2×10⁻¹⁸); and the cleanest design, a promotion holding author and project fixed, still finds acceptance rising +4.1 pp in the same repository at unchanged proposal size (p = 2×10⁻⁷), though even it cannot fully separate the badge from the competence that travels with it. The amplifier's gains are continuous and visible while its costs are rare, delayed and hidden, so hierarchy always appears beneficial and its net cost cannot be honestly settled.

articles.title: Fear and Financial Contagion: Modeling Animal Spirits in Asset Markets
articles.abstract: Using classic equations from cognitive neuroscience, we introduce fear into a model of trading behavior. Fear is modeled as an agent-level state variable with its own autonomic dynamics. Using this framework, we show that financial contagion can emerge as a byproduct of fear-driven responses, by fundamental investors, to negative news. Employing Bayesian model selection techniques, we show that incorporating these dynamic animal spirits substantially improves the model’s fit to real market data relative to standard formulations that exclude fear. Our model also clarifies how fear-driven behavior within one group of investors propagates throughout the market ecosystem via endogenous price feedback.

articles.title: LABOR FORCE PARTICIPATION RATES: DETERMINANTS, TRENDS, AND EMERGING ISSUES ACROSS REGIONS AND DEMOGRAPHICS
articles.authors: Dhias Triwika Rahmadewi, Moses Glorino Rumambo Pandin
articles.abstract: Labor force participation rate (LFPR) is a fundamental indicator of economic vitality and social inclusion. This paper synthesizes recent empirical research on the determinants, trends, and policy implications of labor force participation, drawing on 55 studies published between 1991 and 2026. The review covers demographic, household, cultural, economic, and technological determinants of LFPR, with special attention to regional and gender heterogeneity. Key findings indicate that age, gender, education, marital status, and health are primary drivers of participation, while household composition, cultural norms, and institutional policies mediate these effects. Family-friendly policies such as paid parental leave and affordable childcare have been shown to increase female labor force participation, although their effectiveness is context-dependent. Technological change and the rise of the gig economy are emerging as significant yet under-researched influences, especially in developing regions. This synthesis highlights persistent gender gaps, barriers for married urban women in Southeast Asia, and health-related participation declines among prime-age men in the United States. Future research should address long-term impacts of digitalization, gig work, and health crises on labor force dynamics.

articles.title: The Absorbent Mind: A Study of Parental Awareness at Blue Blocks Montessori School
articles.abstract: This study examines parental understanding of the Montessori concept of the Absorbent Mind through the lens of structured home-based observations conducted across January and February 2026. Drawing on 99 parent-reported observations across two branches of Blue Blocks Montessori School, Hyderabad, the study explores parental awareness across four domains: language, social behaviour, movement, and environmental order. It additionally documents parent-reported intensity of sensitive periods and the consistency of mindful parental behaviour as a form of environmental input. Findings indicate that while most parents demonstrated strong observational awareness across domains, conceptual knowledge of the Absorbent Mind consistently outpaced the practice of sustained mindful engagement with children. Sensitive period intensity showed a clear peak in language and movement. The paper concludes by discussing implications for parent education within Montessori settings.

articles.title: ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE IN PUBLIC FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT: A SYSTEMATIC REVIEW OF READINESS, GOVERNANCE, RISKS, AND APPLICATIONS IN BUDGETING, PROCUREMENT, AND EXPENDITURE MONITORING
articles.abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is increasingly transforming public-sector operations by enhancing data-driven decision-making, automation, and governance capabilities. Within Public Financial Management (PFM), AI applications have emerged in budgeting, procurement, auditing, fraud detection, and expenditure monitoring, offering significant opportunities to improve efficiency, transparency, accountability, and fiscal sustainability. Despite growing interest in AI-enabled governance, existing research remains fragmented across technological, organizational, and governance perspectives. This study addresses this gap by conducting a systematic literature review of AI applications in Public Financial Management, with particular emphasis on budgeting, procurement, and expenditure monitoring. Guided by the PRISMA 2020 framework, the review synthesized 72 studies published between 2015 and 2026 from academic journals, conference proceedings, books, and institutional reports. Thematic analysis identified five major themes: (1) AI applications in Public Financial Management, (2) organizational readiness for AI adoption, (3) algorithmic accountability and responsible AI governance, (4) implementation risks and challenges, and (5) Public Financial Management performance outcomes. Findings indicate that successful AI implementation depends not only on technological capability but also on data readiness, digital infrastructure, human capital, leadership commitment, regulatory preparedness, and robust governance mechanisms. Transparency, explainability, accountability, auditability, ethical compliance, and human oversight emerged as critical determinants of responsible AI adoption. Drawing upon the review findings and Toledo’s (2026) algorithmic accountability framework, the study proposes a conceptual governance framework linking AI readiness, AI adoption, governance mechanisms, risk management, and PFM performance. The framework contributes to the literature by integrating previously fragmented research streams and provides policymakers and practitioners with a structured approach for the responsible deployment of AI in government financial systems.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Behavioral Determinants of Credit Score Improvement and the Role of Structured Credit Counseling in Millennial Debt Recovery
articles.abstract: This study, informed by the practitioner expertise of Antwaine Bullocks, known as the Credit Genius, investigates the behavioral determinants of credit score improvement among millennial consumers in the United States and evaluates the role of structured credit counseling programs in accelerating debt recovery outcomes within this demographic. Millennials represent the largest generational cohort in the American workforce yet exhibit disproportionately low credit scores and elevated levels of consumer debt. Drawing on a mixed-methods framework integrating survey data from 312 millennial respondents aged 22 to 36 with qualitative interviews conducted at Rapid Credit Boosters, a San Diego-based credit counseling organization, this paper identifies financial self-efficacy, payment consistency, and credit utilization awareness as primary behavioral predictors of score improvement. Counseled participants recorded an average FICO score improvement of 74 points over six months compared to 31 points among non-counseled peers. The study concludes that coach-led, individualized credit counseling targeting behavioral and psychological dimensions of credit management constitutes an effective and scalable pathway to financial inclusion for the millennial generation.

articles.title: Administrative Trauma and Performativity: Reconciling Identity, Ethics, Morals, and Pragmatics across Operational Strata During Complex Humanitarian Emergencies
articles.authors: Amaya Alexandra Ramos, MSW, CPH, MA
articles.abstract: As in many professions, human-focused administrative personnel must balance public perception with both safety and desirable service outcomes. Those with high-level, yet ultimately intermediary coordinating roles, have the added pressure of unifying interest blocs situated across varying operational strata, each moving at its own tempo and in accordance with its own objectives. At times, these diverse priorities may even be in direct contradiction. However, while such brokering may be natural to the role, successful management of complex humanitarian emergencies requires extreme clarity of focus and vision, and as such, there is a heightened emphasis on harmonizing operations towards these larger objectives at all costs, often doing so in environments that are under-resourced in terms of information, materials, manpower, training, and political support. In addition to these baseline stressors, public administrators specifically carry the performance pressure of serving as proxies for a large, optics-and ethics-sensitive social apparatus —the government— whose operations must be upheld through careful calibration of the social contract. In the course of emergency response, such personnel must therefore navigate traumatic situations that may violate aspects of their deeply held personal beliefs, all while strictly managing personal affect. These cognitive, ethical, and moral reconciliation processes can be intensely draining, and without a minimum amount of decision making authority combined with specialized logistical and emotional support, specialized administrative personnel may be at risk for a unique and intense kind of moral injury stemming from unremitting crisis combined with suppression of their instinct for advocacy. Via this lens, and in the interest of calling for greater literature on the humanitarian political economy, as well as administrative experiences within migration health research, this report briefly recounts various aspects of the administrative moral injury borne by United States public servants and similar personnel during the 2021 evacuation of eligible parties from Afghanistan following the U.S. military withdrawal from the country and the ensuing Taliban takeover.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: The Humane Gate: A Scientific Blueprint for Algorithmic Gatekeeping in Social Media Platforms
articles.abstract: Algorithmic gatekeeping is the process by which computational systems select, rank, suppress, label, recommend, or route information before it reaches users. This paper develops a comprehensive descriptive research blueprint for any future social media platform that must govern attention, safety, credibility, creator opportunity, public relevance, and user autonomy. The proposed mechanism, called the Multi-Layer Algorithmic Gatekeeping Ecosystem (MAGE), treats gatekeeping as a measurable socio-technical process rather than a hidden ranking trick. MAGE combines content integrity scoring, social graph context, creator reputation, user intent, epistemic value, harm risk, fairness constraints, diversity exposure, machine-learning classifiers, large language models, agentic AI review assistants, human review, appeal rights, audit logs, and public accountability. The paper provides mathematical definitions, optimization objectives, pseudocode tables, descriptive research variables, simulated platform scenarios, validation procedures, distributed microservice designs, and out-of-the-box implementation modules such as pluralistic feeds, friction gates, civic-mode routing, provenance markets, deliberation chambers, epistemic nutrition labels, and user-controlled gatekeeping sliders. The contribution is both a formal theory of humane epistemic gatekeeping and a platform-ready governance architecture that can be implemented before launch, studied descriptively after launch, and audited continuously as the platform evolves.

articles.title: When the Compound Speaks: Documenting the Transformation of Indigenous Igbo Architectural Identity in Anambra State, Nigeria.
articles.abstract: The Igbo compound is not simply a collection of buildings arranged around a shared space. It is a spatial argument about how the world is ordered, how authority flows through a family, how the living maintain their relationship with the ancestors and how a community understands itself in relation to the land it occupies. At the centre of that argument stands the obi, the reception house of the compound head, whose physical form, interior arrangement and ritual significance together constitute one of the most culturally dense architectural types in West Africa. This article reports on original ethnographic fieldwork conducted across multiple communities in Anambra State, Nigeria, as part of the EUNIC/Goethe-Institut Nigeria Roots and Roofs Programme. Seven community members were interviewed in person, including elders, obi custodians, engineers, a former community president general and a traditional ruler who serves as Chairman of the Anambra State Traditional Rulers Council. Their testimony, presented here with direct quotation from interview transcripts, documents the nature and extent of the architectural transformation that Igbo domestic space has undergone over the past generation, the materials and cosmological knowledge being lost in that transition, and the enduring significance of the obi as a spatial, social and spiritual institution. The article draws on this primary evidence alongside the scholarly literature on Igbo architecture and spatial culture to argue that what is being lost in the shift from earthen, open and cosmologically grounded compound forms to walled, cement-built modern houses is not merely a matter of materials or aesthetics but of the social and spiritual fabric of community life itself. It calls for the systematic documentation of surviving traditional compounds and obi structures, the integration of vernacular architecture study into Nigerian building technology education, and a design culture that takes seriously the spatial wisdom embedded in the Igbo compound tradition.

articles.title: Gastos Municipais em Saúde e Anos Potenciais de Vida Perdidos: Uma Análise em Painel para os Municípios Brasileiros
articles.authors: Jorge Luis Tonetto, Gustavo Inácio de Moraes
articles.abstract: Esta pesquisa insere-se no campo da economia da saúde e analisa se os gastos públicos municipais em saúde influenciam o indicador de Anos Potenciais de Vida Perdidos (APVP) nos municípios brasileiros. Os resultados indicam evidências de que os gastos municipais em saúde estão associados à melhoria do indicador APVP, com destaque para os gastos em atenção básica, que se mostraram estatisticamente significativos, enquanto os gastos em assistência hospitalar não apresentaram significância estatística. As estimativas também indicam relação não linear entre gasto e resultado em saúde, sugerindo oportunidade na elevação seletiva dos gastos que se mostraram abaixo do ponto estimado de maior eficiência, especialmente na atenção básica. A análise foi realizada para o período de 2005 a 2014, considerando os municípios brasileiros com dados disponíveis em bases administrativas nacionais. O APVP foi calculado para cada município e ano, adotando-se 70 anos como limite de vida potencial. Empregou-se modelo econométrico com dados em painel e estimativas robustas, utilizando duas especificações: uma baseada na função saúde e outra nas subfunções atenção básica e assistência hospitalar. Os resultados também indicam melhora gradual no desempenho dos municípios ao longo dos períodos de gestão e sugerem ganhos de escala associados ao tamanho populacional dos municípios. English This research is situated within the field of health economics and analyzes whether municipal public health expenditures influence the indicator of Potential Years of Life Lost (PYLL) in Brazilian municipalities. The results provide evidence that municipal health expenditures are associated with improvements in the PYLL indicator, with particular emphasis on primary health care expenditures, which were statistically significant, while hospital care expenditures did not show statistical significance. The estimates also indicate a nonlinear relationship between spending and health outcomes, suggesting opportunities for selective increases in expenditures that remain below the estimated point of greater efficiency, particularly in primary health care. The analysis covers the period from 2005 to 2014 and includes Brazilian municipalities with available data from national administrative databases. PYLL was calculated for each municipality and year, adopting 70 years as the potential life limit. An econometric panel data model with robust estimates was employed, using two specifications: one based on the health function and another based on the subfunctions of primary care and hospital care. The results also indicate gradual improvements in municipal performance over successive administrative periods and suggest economies of scale associated with municipal population size.

articles.title: TRACE: An Interactive AI and Compliance-by-Design Framework for Financial Crime Detection
articles.abstract: The use of artificial intelligence is growing in the domain of cyber-enabled financial crime detection and has created a significant accountability gap: AI models have been increasingly influencing the regulatory compliance outcomes, and yet their logic is opaque to the humans who are experts and bear legal responsibility for decisions (International Monetary Fund, 2026; Radončić, 2026). Instead of designing AI models to be transparent from their foundation, institutions add the layer of post-hoc explanations onto the autonomous or semi-autonomous AI models (European Commission, 2021; FATF, 2021). Recent research on interactive AI shows analysts prefer to interact and be actively involved with the systems rather than passively consume the outputs (Raees et al., 2024; Wang, 2019). This paper argues that, in high-risk domains such as anti-money laundering, cyber-enabled financial fraud, and cybercrimes, AI should function as a rule-based and human-in-the-loop decision-support system which structures evidence and applies typologies, rules, and auditable variables to support risk assessments while retaining human judgement for final decisions (Bank for International Settlements, 2025; Fink, 2025). This paper introduces TRACE (Transaction Risk Analysis and Compliance Engine) as an interactive AI; it is the manifestation of the compliance-by-design principle. TRACE integrates a transparent rule-based support engine and mandatory human analysts’ approval into a single compliance-grade architecture. In this design, the AI only suggests the risk scoring and flags up factors. To provide support for analysts to make decisions without compromising on transparency, TRACE integrates an auditable retrieval module that draws upon the curated regulated knowledge base, such as FATF, RBI guidelines, and national KYC derivatives. Upon analyst request, contextual evidence, and surfacing retrieved snippets with reliable sources, it remains separate from rule-based scoring or risk narratives (Johnson et al., 2021; Lewis et al., 2020; Saison Technology, 2026). The grounds on which TRACE operates are using the foundational principles of ethics and responsible AI. The risk alerts are flagged using transparency and precise regulatory standards with the aim of mitigating demographic bias in compliance assessments. As it strictly functions as a compliance support tool and not as any enforcement system, it reflects a clear commitment towards responsible AI in this domain, where errors carry serious consequences (AML RightSource, 2024; European Commission, 2021).

articles.title: Consequence-Indexed Autonomy: Calibrating AI Restraint by Data Classification in Regulated Knowledge Work
articles.abstract: In regulated knowledge work—federal training, accredited credentialing, and any workflow that touches controlled information—the prevailing reflex ties an AI agent’s autonomy to its demon‐ strated capability. This paper argues that the reflex is backwards: the ceiling on autonomy should be indexed to the consequence space of a task—its data classification, together with the reach and reversibility of its output—and set independently of, and as a hard upper bound on, capability. Capability does not determine the limit; consequence does. The contribution is not the idea that autonomy should track stakes, which the 2025–26 agentic-governance literature already holds in pieces, but its assembly into an operational instrument. The paper (1) decomposes governance readiness and regulatory restraint into two conceptually independent axes combined by the rule operating point = min(readiness, restraint-cap); (2) builds a data-classification-to-autonomy-ceiling instrument across the U.S. federal regime—public, internal, CUI, classified, and ITAR-controlled—each rung reasoned from its controlling author‐ ity, with an auditable reach-and-reversibility modifier; and (3) demonstrates the rule on a federal instructional-design workflow in which the consequence ceiling cuts below an organiza‐ tion’s readiness. The instrument composes beneath risk-based regimes (the NIST AI RMF and the EU AI Act) rather than competing with them. It extends a published framework’s Proportion‐ al Restraint Scale and is offered as a governance-design specification inviting field validation.

articles.title: Intellectualization After Humiliation: A Bayesian, Computational, and Media-Theoretic Research Framework
articles.abstract: Intellectualization is a defensive pattern in which painful affect is translated into analysis, theory, diagnosis, or procedural explanation before it can be felt. This paper reframes intellectualization after humiliation as a measurable, mediated, and socially distributed phenomenon rather than a purely intrapsychic habit. The argument is deliberately interdisciplinary: contemporary defense-mechanism research supplies the psychological grounding; shame, ostracism, and workplace mistreatment literatures explain the social injury; computational social science supplies text, voice, interview, and platform-based measurement; Bayesian modeling supplies principled uncertainty; and media studies explains how public discourse, platform affordances, visibility, and rhetorical performance shape the way people convert humiliation into explanation. The paper proposes a platformized research design in which remote interviews, diary entries, speech samples, surveys, and social-context metadata are fused through Bayesian updating and frequentist validation. It also gives an R implementation strategy for automated analysis using text mining, acoustic features, mixed-effects regression, and Bayesian multilevel models. The central claim is that intellectualization should be detected not by isolated jargon, but by a convergent pattern: high abstraction, low direct affect, low bodily reference, high causal overproduction, and contextual evidence of shame threat.

articles.title: Is there a Simple Explanation for the Kraemer Effect?
articles.abstract: People tend to judge that an agent brought about the ends more intentionally than the means that brought about those ends. This difference in intentionality judgments is called the Kraemer Effect. We aimed to determine whether there was a simple explanation for the Kraemer Effect. Recent work found that people judge an action to be intentional to the extent that the agent’s mental state caused the action (Quillien and German, 2021). In line with this work, we hypothesized that people show the Kraemer Effect because they think the agent’s desire was more causal in bringing about the ends than the means. In two experiments, we tested this hypothesis. While we replicated the Kraemer Effect in both experiments, we found no evidence in support of this explanation for the Kraemer Effect.

articles.title: Risk Environments of Postsocialism. Sex Work, Drug Policy, and HIV in Eastern and Southeastern Europe Between War, Criminalisation, and External Defunding. A Theoretical Synthesis.
articles.abstract: This paper develops a theoretical synthesis of sex work, drug policy and HIV in Eastern and Southeastern Europe - a region that fits neither the governance typology of the Global North nor the self-organisation typology of the Global South developed in two companion papers. The region's defining feature is the intersection of sex work with injecting drug use under double criminalisation, governed in much of the post-Soviet space not by criminal law in the strict sense but by a distinct administrative-offence regime inherited from the late Soviet period. Eastern Europe and Central Asia is, alongside the Middle East/North Africa and Latin America, one of three world regions where new HIV infections have risen rather than fallen since 2010. Two axes structure the analysis. The first traces the postsocialist legal-epidemiological complex from its administrative-law foundations through Tim Rhodes' risk environment framework - developed in this region - to the catastrophic 2014 Crimean methadone ban and the contrasting resilience of Ukraine's wartime opioid agonist treatment system. The second traces an inverted extraterritorial axis: where the Northern paper found US power exercised through platform law and the Southern paper through the PEPFAR pledge, this region experiences the abrupt 2025 withdrawal of PEPFAR/USAID funding colliding with an indigenous Russian counter-power - 'traditional values' politics and foreign-agent legislation that actively suppresses sex worker self-organisation (SWAN, Silver Rose, Legalife-Ukraine) rather than merely leaving it unfunded. A cross-cutting axis examines Southeastern Europe and intra-EU mobility, where Romania's collapse from Global Fund eligibility to antiretroviral supply crises shows treatment access becoming a function of residency and funding status rather than medical need - a pattern this paper extends to the underexamined situation of HIV-positive sex workers who return from Western Europe to Romania or Bulgaria. The paper argues that the postsocialist risk environment constitutes a fourth, structurally distinct case: neither self-built protective infrastructure as in the South nor institutionalised labour-law normalisation as in the North, but a terrain where both state and self-organised protection remain precarious against external shocks - war, funding withdrawal, and authoritarian repression alike. articles.title: The Red Queen at the Grassroots: The Co-Evolution of Cadre Resistance and Regime Control
articles.abstract: Why do Chinese grassroots cadres sometimes voluntarily report their own disciplinary violations, triggering investigations that suspend their impossible policy tasks? And what happens when the disciplinary system learns to strike back? Drawing on 32 in-depth interviews with current and former cadres in S City, H Province, combined with official statistics and policy documents (2020–2026), this paper documents an emerging phenomenon:"cadre self-informing",and traces its evolution from a rational risk-avoidance strategy to a failing one. Self-informing becomes rational under a triple condition: fiscal depletion (local self-sufficiency rate below 35%), incentive inversion (the penalty for "incompetence" outweighs that for "corruption"), and the collapse of social trust. Facing zero-budget demolition tasks and catastrophic sanctions for failure, cadres strategically exploit a procedural rigidity,that every disciplinary report must be investigated,to pause the implementation clock and seek refuge in a bounded, predictable investigation rather than an unbounded, career-ending charge of incompetence. Yet the system does not remain inert. Through longitudinal tracking of a key case,a cadre whose self-informing strategy survived a traditional investigation but unraveled spectacularly under an AI-enhanced one,this paper reveals that the disciplinary apparatus is itself a learning entity. AI-driven tools compressed investigation timelines, shattered the informational opacity "battle-tested" cadres had relied upon, and fundamentally recalibrated the risk calculus of self-informing. Drawing on the Red Queen effect, this paper reconceptualizes the "execution limit" of China's pressure-based system not as a static boundary, but as a moving front in an ongoing arms race between agents and the institutions that discipline them. The findings advance institutional gap theory beyond state-society contention into internal regime-agent dynamics, and identify a "high-pressure trap" in adaptive authoritarianism: over-mobilization drains the very resources and trust that adaptability requires, generating the evasion it seeks to extinguish.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: A Stylized Computable General Equilibrium Model for Circular Economy Strategy Analysis
articles.abstract: This work presents a stylized computable general equilibrium (CGE) model for analysing circular economy policy strategies in an intentionally abstract setting. The model is not calibrated to a specific economy; instead, it uses round-number calibration data and systematic numerical experiments to identify the behavioural and physical conditions under which circular strategies reduce primary material use, represented by virgin metal. The analysis compares virgin-metal taxation, recycling support, and life-extension support through refurbishment, repair, and reuse in single-country and two-country model variants. The results show that circular strategies cannot be ranked by label alone. Virgin-metal taxation operates through an upstream material-price channel and is comparatively insensitive to how end-of-life products are allocated among circular routes. Recycling and life-extension support depend more strongly on collection and allocation of end-of-life products, route yields, recycled-metal quality, route substitutability, and final-product service-demand response. For some combinations of demand elasticity, substitution elasticities, route yields, and recycled-metal quality, a policy can reduce virgin-metal use even while demand for the final product service increases; for others, the same demand response can weaken or reverse the expected primary-material reduction. Support-efficiency comparisons show that refurbishment support performs better than recycling support in the closed-economy case, while this gap narrows when virgin-metal production is separated from consumption and circular processing in the two-country extension. The nested life-extension analysis further shows that refurbishment, repair, and reuse are not interchangeable policy mechanisms. Overall, the model provides a reproducible equilibrium framework for identifying where circular economy strategies work, where they fail, and which assumptions drive the difference.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: From Digital Public Infrastructure to AI Governance: Lessons from India for the Global South
articles.abstract: Digital Public Infrastructure, or DPI, has become the model that governments across the Global South are told to copy. India's Aadhaar identity system and its Unified Payments Interface sit at the center of that story. Now that the same governments are confronting artificial intelligence, a second claim has attached itself to the first: that whatever institutional strength built India's DPI should also help a state govern AI. This paper puts that claim to the test instead of taking it on faith. It compares India, Brazil, Indonesia and Kenya using a most-differentsystems design, tracing six candidate mechanisms through which DPI experience might generate AI governance capacity, to ask where that capacity actually comes from. Three rival hypotheses are weighed against the record: direct institutional transfer, technological discontinuity, and conditional adaptation. The evidence favours the third. DPI-derived capacity does carry over, but only in part, and only once it meets independent regulators, enforceable data-protection law and accountability mechanisms that DPI programmes do not generate on their own. To keep the claim testable, the paper treats AI governance capacity as a disaggregated construct covering regulatory, enforcement, technical, coordination, accountability, data-governance and compute capacity, and builds a DPI-to-AI Governance Framework that specifies when transfer works and when it does not. It ends with what this means for governments drawn to the "India model," and with an agenda for the comparative and quantitative work that should follow.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Beyond Agency Lists Regulatory Stewardship and System Visibility in New Zealand and the Australian Commonwealth
articles.abstract: The abstract argues that a fair Trans-Tasman comparison should examine New Zealand’s unitary national regulatory system against the Australian Commonwealth’s national regulatory architecture, rather than comparing New Zealand with the entire Australian federation. Using the Seven-Layer Regulatory Architecture, it shows that both systems contain similar layers of regulatory authority, including statutory regulators, departmental units, professional bodies, tribunals, cross-jurisdictional arrangements and regulatory dark matter. The main difference is organisational: New Zealand is more compact and coherent, while the Australian Commonwealth is denser and more dispersed across portfolios, statutory bodies, schemes and low-visibility mechanisms. The wider Australian federation then amplifies this density into greater fragmentation. The abstract concludes that regulatory reform should start with architecture-level visibility, not simple agency lists, and that stewardship must focus on where authority, capability, informal practice and system learning actually sit.

articles.title: From Efficiency to Sufficiency: A Measurement-Informed Framework for Cost-Aware Automated Essay Scoring
articles.abstract: Compute cost in automated essay scoring (AES) is usually treated as a technical efficiency issue rather than a measurement design concern. Yet deployment decisions require more than reporting latency, model size, or inference expense. In high-stakes settings, total burden includes validation, monitoring, adjudication, compliance, and governance; in instructional settings, pricing, licensing, and integration may dominate cost. This paper reframes AES as a cost-constrained measurement system and introduces a four-layer framework—metering, allocation, unit economics, and decision—for translating raw computational signals into decision-relevant cost constructs. It defines cost per valid score (CPVS) and argues that AES systems should be compared by quality-cost sufficiency: minimizing cost among defensible scoring designs that satisfy measurement requirements. The framework provides a portable basis for representing, comparing, and evaluating cost across AES studies and deployment contexts.

articles.title: Algorithmic Ageism and the Governance of Later Life: How Automated Decision-Making Systems Reshape Inequality Among Older Adults
articles.abstract: Automated decision-making systems increasingly mediate access to welfare benefits, healthcare, employment, and financial services. While sociological scholarship has critically examined algorithmic bias in relation to race, gender, and class, age remains under-theorized. This paper introduces the concept of algorithmic ageism to describe how automated systems reproduce and intensify age-based inequalities through data-driven governance. Drawing on sociological theories of ageism, surveillance, and governmentality, the paper analyzes how later life is rendered governable through algorithmic classification, risk scoring, and prediction. It argues that automated decision-making systems do not merely reflect existing age inequalities but actively reshape the governance of later life by embedding normative assumptions about productivity, risk, and dependency. The paper concludes by discussing the implications of algorithmic ageism for social inequality, democratic accountability, and the regulation of automated systems.

articles.title: Sibling Correlations and SES Overlaps in Late-Career Swedes
articles.abstract: To what extent is socioeconomic standing (SES) uni- or multidimensional? SES is typically measured using variables such as education, occupation, income, and, more recently, wealth, but do these variables reflect the same or different hierarchies? This paper uses the methodology of Karlson and Birkelund (2025) to study how sibling correlations, as a measure of the role of family background, overlap across SES dimensions. Four dimensions of SES are considered: education, occupation, income (earnings and disposable income), and wealth. The findings reveal three clusters in the overlap pattern of sibling correlations across these dimensions: (1) education/occupation, (2) income, and (3) wealth. This pattern remains consistent across subsamples defined by sex. The analysis shows that a unidimensional sibling factor, assuming a single latent SES factor, fails to capture the observed sibling correlations adequately. Still, the unidimensional factor accounts for modest to substantial portions of the sibling correlations across outcomes. The evidence for a single latent family factor weakens further when additional outcomes such as crime, cognitive ability, and physical capacity (health) are included in a younger male sample born between 1958 and 1968. A life-course extension shows substantial, though incomplete, stability in the family characteristics shaping income across early-, mid-, and late-career stages. In conclusion, while a unidimensional SES factor exists, it is substantively dominated by multidimensionality in intergenerational transmission.

articles.title: Where DESO Disappears: Spatial Inequality and Social Stratification at Labour Market Entry
articles.abstract: At country-level, a host of evidence suggests there is a sizeable direct effect of social origin (DESO) on initial labour market outcomes, net of educational attainment. What is true at country-level is not always true below country-level, however. Using data from the UK Household Longitudinal Survey and the German Socio-Economic Panel, we show that variable spatial opportunity structures moderate the size of DESO at labour market entry, such that there are places where DESO disappears. Social origins assume greater importance as local labour market conditions deteriorate: in weak local labour markets, non-graduates are approximately 16 percentage points less likely to find employment if their parents are care workers rather than secondary school teachers, while graduates typically obtain first jobs that are 7–9 ISEI points lower in status. These findings highlight the distinctive geography of social stratification processes at labour market entry and potentially beyond.

articles.title: Fishing Sector Perspectives: Perceived Threats in the Polish Baltic Sea Fleet by Vessel Size and Target Species
articles.authors: Michael J. Rosciszewski-Dodgson, Giuseppe T. Cirella
articles.abstract: As ecological and economic pressures mount in the Baltic Sea, commercial fisheries face increasing challenges. This study investigates fishermen’s perceptions of four major threats—climate change, pollution, overfishing, and marine predators—with attention to vessel size (fishing boat, cutter, supercutter) and target species (demersal, mixed, small pelagic).
