Based on 129 surveys and 28 interviews across Poland, the analysis shows that while all threats are acknowledged, perceptions vary significantly by operational context. Seventy-eight coded interview quotations illustrate how sector-specific experiences shape these views, underscoring the need to integrate them into fisheries governance. Friedman test results (p < 0.001) confirm significant variation across threat categories, with the strongest contrasts for overfishing and predator concerns. Smaller vessels targeting demersal and mixed stocks identified predators as the primary threat, while supercutters and pelagic operators emphasized quota restrictions and international competition. Climate change and pollution were viewed as less immediate, though some reported localized impacts. Incorporating sector perspectives is essential for adaptive, sustainable fisheries management.

articles.title: Turning Pages into Screens: Media Adaptation and the Reclassification of Cultural Objects
articles.abstract: When a cultural object moves from one medium to another, does it get reclassified, and does that reclassification change demand? We argue that it does, through a mechanism we call medium transposition: audiences evaluate objects by the conventions of the medium in front of them, so crossing media can shift the category in which an object is placed and reshape the demand that follows. We test this argument with Heartstopper, a queer young-adult graphic-novel series adapted by Netflix in April 2022, combining NielsenIQ BookData weekly sales across eleven national markets with 2,981 Amazon book reviews and 874 IMDb viewer reviews. The Netflix adaptation triggered a discontinuous increase of approximately 76,000 weekly book sales, largest in markets with dense local publishing infrastructure and running predominantly through local-language translation editions. Both audiences are responding to the same underlying story, yet they talk about it in strikingly different ways: Amazon reviewers focus on gifting and intergenerational circulation, while IMDb reviewers focus on LGBTQ+ visibility and self-recognition, a gap of 3.9 to one in representation language across platforms. A within-Amazon comparison, holding platform constant, shows that family-gift language rises and identitarian queer language declines after the Season 1 release, suggesting that the classification shift is tied to the medium crossing rather than only to differences between the two review platforms. Medium transposition offers a portable account of rapid, infrastructure-dependent, audience-driven reclassification, one that requires no shift in taste and no campaign by critics. The price, visible in the review evidence, is that expansion and dilution are not easily separated: the same process that routes Heartstopper through mainstream gatekeepers as ordinary teenage fiction also reduces the share of identitarian queer language in the discourse that surrounds it.

articles.title: Settlement, Return, or Onward Migration? An Intersectional Perspective on Migration Decisions
articles.authors: Katia Gallegos Torres, Agnieszka Kanas, Yuliya Kosyakova
articles.abstract: Out-migration is a central yet understudied dimension of migration processes, with important implications for labor supply, integration trajectories, and population composition. This paper examines how out-migration intentions vary across migrant groups and how economic ties, socio-cultural embeddedness, and transnational ties are associated with return and onward migration. Using recent data on immigrants in Germany, the analysis shows that out-migration intentions are shaped by multiple mechanisms rather than a single dominant factor. Socio-cultural embeddedness is the strongest correlate of lower mobility, whereas economic resources and transnational ties have more heterogeneous associations. Return and onward migration are linked to distinct configurations of attachments, and these patterns vary systematically by gender and migration motive. Men are more likely to report onward migration, and economic migrants exhibit higher overall mobility than family migrants and refugees. Overall, the findings highlight the importance of distinguishing between forms of mobility and moving beyond purely economic explanations to better understand migrant retention and mobility dynamics.

articles.title: TRANSCEND-T2D-1 and the First-Line Question: What Retatrutide's Phase 3 Results Mean for Diabetes Care, Access & Equity
articles.abstract: The TRANSCEND-T2D-1 trial establishes retatrutide as the first triple agonist for type 2 diabetes. Over 40 weeks, 537 adults achieved placebo-subtracted HbA1c reductions of 0.88%–1.12% and 8.9%–12.7% (20–29 lbs) weight loss, exceeding tirzepatide's weight loss in SURPASS-1 and rivaling bariatric surgery. Yet retatrutide's efficacy comes with higher adverse events (discontinuation 2.5% overall, 5.1% for 12 mg vs. 0% placebo), a projected annual cost of $12,000, and a budget impact ICER estimates would exceed $880 million annually with <1% eligible patients treated. This creates a hidden equity mechanism: patients with paid sick leave can navigate dose escalation; those without cannot. Existing GLP-1 disparities forewarn. Non-Hispanic Blacks have 28% lower odds of GLP-1 prescription, and Black and Hispanic patients face lower out-of-pocket costs and lower fill rates. An unregulated gray market already flourishes: 15 pounds of retatrutide entered the U.S. between Jan–Apr 2026, with 14 hospitalizations reported. Three policy interventions, CMS Coverage with Evidence Development, FDA post-marketing diversity requirements, and state Medicaid VBP, could improve access if implemented before FDA approval, expected within 12-18 months. The next 18 months will determine whether this breakthrough succeeds or becomes a marker of who can afford the best care.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Is it all about the costs? Cultural distance and adjustment of recent migrants
articles.authors: Andreas Genoni, Jean Philippe Décieux , Elke Murdock
articles.abstract: The cultural distance (CD) between the origin and destination country of migrants has long been considered important for individual adjustment. Past research predominantly followed a cost perspective, emphasising rising adjustment costs with increasing CD and postulating a negative linear relationship. Previous studies therefore overlooked potential benefits of CD for adjustment. We address this gap by taking a more differentiated perspective on CD and adjustment. Using probability-based survey data on recent German migrants , we examined migrant adjustment at different CD-levels. Employing multilevel regressions for sociocultural and psychological adjustment, the findings reveal an inverted-U shaped relationship between CD and both adjustment dimensions. This challenges the cost perspective, indicating an “optimal” range of intermediate CD as most beneficial for adjustment. Thus, the prevailing view on negative consequences of CD for adjustment may underestimate the challenges for culturally close migrants, and overlook that certain levels of cultural dissimilarity can facilitate migrants’ adjustment.

articles.title: Disfungsi Pengawasan Badan Permusyawaratan Desa: Subordinasi Struktural, Asimetri Informasi, dan Desain Kelembagaan Alternatif
articles.abstract: The Village Consultative Council (BPD) was designed under Law 6/2014, amended by Law 3/2024, as an institutional counterweight to the village head. In practice, it operates in dysfunction due to structural subordination and fiscal dependency. Through policy analysis of Law 3/2024, GP 16/2026, and MoHA Regulations 110/2016 and 20/2018, this article identifies seven design failures, including budget dependency, recruitment vulnerabilities, weak enforcement, and information asymmetry. Comparative analysis with local councils in India, Bangladesh, and the Philippines reveals that oversight independence requires protected budgets and proactive information access. This article recommends three policy innovations via regional discretion without statutory amendment: a protected budget mechanism (10–15% of the Village Fund Allocation), a proactive right-to-receive information framework, and an institutionalized Village Social Audit Forum integrated with regional inspectorates and measured through a Village Democracy Health Index (VDHI). Keywords: village governance, BPD oversight, institutional design, social audit, Village Fund Allocation

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Aspirational Mathematics and the Behavioral Sciences: The Columbia Behavioral Models Project, 1952–1957
articles.abstract: The Behavioral Models project (1952–1957) at Columbia University had a clear mission: to make mathematics legible to social scientists. The project, funded by the Office of Naval Research (ONR), enlisted mathematicians and philosophers to demonstrate how mathematical models could be applied to the study of human behavior. Measured against this aim, the project was a failure. The flavors of mathematical modeling promoted by the project were rarely adopted by mainstream social scientists, the target audience. If anything, the disciplines embraced (or re-embraced) the correlational tradition of inferential statistics—an approach that the project had defined itself against. The paper argues that the Behavioral Models Project (BMP) is an expression of aspirational mathematics. The postwar behavioral sciences movement imagined itself, in a gauzy, rhetorical sense, in the image of the natural sciences. But its more proximate reference discipline was economics, which was already self-segregating by the early 1950s. Economists’ mix of mathematical rigor and policy influence, on early and notably partial display in these years, made their discipline an object of envy and qualified emulation. The BMP—framed as a maths booster shot for the behavioral sciences—was a high-profile index of that dynamic. Mathematical sophistication, if not necessarily the economists’ variety, was something to cultivate. As the BMP case helps to show, the aspiration went unfulfilled.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Focused MAIHDA: Studying Intersectional Inequalities through the Lens of a Focal Social Identity
articles.abstract: Intersectional MAIHDA (Multilevel Analysis of Individual Heterogeneity and Discriminatory Accuracy) is a widely used approach in quantitative intersectional research for exploring how mean outcomes vary across combinations of social identity characteristics, such as gender, race, and social class. However, in many studies, researchers are especially interested in a particular identity, such as gender, operationalised (imperfectly) by sex. This gives rise to additional questions that cannot be addressed using standard MAIHDA: Does the magnitude and importance of intersectional inequalities differ between men and women? If so, who experiences greater inequality? To what extent do additive patterns of intersectional inequality align across the sexes, and where do they differ? Are departures from these general patterns, reflecting unique intersectional experiences, more pronounced for one sex than the other? Finally, at the level of specific strata, where do the largest sex differences emerge? To address these questions, we introduce a focused-lens approach to MAIHDA that enables researchers to investigate situations in which sex—or any other social identity—is of particular interest. In doing so, we expand the range of questions that MAIHDA can address and thereby broaden its contribution to quantitative intersectional research. We motivate and illustrate this approach by revisiting the seminal BMI MAIHDA application of Evans et al. (2018).

articles.title: Rethinking Certification Culture in Procurement and Supply Chain: Why Capability Must Replace Credentialism in the Age of AI
articles.abstract: Certification bodies have long shaped the professional landscape of procurement and supply chain, offering standardized knowledge and global recognition. Yet the increasing reliance on theoretical exams, recurring fees, and credential centric hiring has created structural challenges that limit inclusivity, undervalue experience, and fail to reflect the realities of modern practice. This paper examines the limitations of certification driven evaluation, the social and economic pressures it creates, and the widening gap between certification standards and real world capability — especially as artificial intelligence now performs many tasks traditionally tested in certification exams. Rather than dismissing certification bodies, the paper proposes a shift toward capability based, evidence driven, AI enhanced validation that honors practical judgment, ethical decision making, and lived professional experience. The goal is reform, not rejection: a modernized model where certification bodies evolve from exam providers into capability verification authorities.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: The Structural Failure of Passive Observation and Recovery by Source-Independent Action: Minimal Results in a Finite, Elementary Setting
articles.abstract: Using only elementary finite mathematics—the pigeonhole principle and the finite law of total probability, with no causal graph, no measure theory, and no axiom of infinity—we characterise when passive observation suffices to predict interventions and when it does not, obtaining three results. First, impossibility: every bounded representation f : X → Z with |X| > |Z| satisfying a sufficient-statistic condition admits a pair of environments that are observationally identical yet interventionally distinct, on which every passive strategy fails at every sample size. The obstruction performs no arithmetic on probabilities—distributions enter only through equality and its negation, so it holds verbatim with distributions replaced by abstract labels; arithmetic enters exactly once, a single inequality converting the failure into error floors uniform in the sample size. Second, sufficiency: under a source-invariance condition on the outcome mechanism, any non-adaptive policy that draws each action value from a source independent of the latent state recovers the interventional distribution by a single finite sum. Third, necessity: among fixed assignment sources, source independence is exactly the condition for recovery uniform over outcome mechanisms, and every dependent source manufactures the same two-point obstruction, with worst-case error equal to the dependence of the source. Together these give a tight two-sided characterisation: source-independent action is necessary and sufficient for recovery, a dependent source is as uninformative as passive observation, and source independence is what separates failure from recovery. The action operation is graph-free, coinciding with Pearl's do on acyclic structural causal models and extending to simultaneous-equilibrium environments on which do is undefined.

articles.title: Notes on the categorial-logic of Capital
articles.abstract: The validity of the “method applied in Capital” as an external toolkit is one of the main issues that follows the publication of the first volume in 1867. The proposal presented here is based on the premise that this problem rests on a basic formal misunderstanding of the Marxian legacy. The hypothesis herein is as follows: the presentation found in Capital imposes that the method must conform to the object by the latter’s necessity.

articles.title: Challengers Shift to Lower-Tier Competition When Elite Incumbents Extend Their Tenure: Evidence From Professional Tennis
articles.abstract: Extended incumbent tenure is hard to study empirically because dominant incumbents and weaker successors may simply differ in talent. We study this using professional tennis, where the Big Four—Federer, Nadal, Djokovic, and Murray—remained at the summit 5–10 years beyond the historical norm. For 296 players with fully elapsed prime windows, we construct a tenure extension exposure index measuring how far past historical norms the Big Four were during each player’s prime. Controlling for pre-prime talent, higher exposure predicts fewer main-tour matches, more Challenger-circuit matches, and earlier exit from the world top 100 once secular trends are absorbed—consistent with downward substitution from elite to sub-elite competition. Raw Big Four dominance shows the opposite pattern, indicating the effect is specific to extended tenure, not opponent quality. Talented athletes are not simply outcompeted but redirected to lower-visibility, lower-revenue tiers, with implications for tournament design, athlete welfare, and succession planning where senior careers are lengthening.

articles.title: South Korea’s Nuclear-Energy Entanglements and the Timescales of Ecological Democracy
articles.abstract: This chapter analyzes controversies surrounding nuclear-energy infrastructure in South Korea, where the density of nuclear reactors is the highest in the world. Focusing on the city of Kyŏngju – a UNESCO World Heritage site closely associated with both South Korean cultural patrimony and the authoritarian developmentalism that drove the expansion of the country’s nuclear-energy program – it asks how democratic thinking and practice reckon with the inheritance of energy infrastructure from the military-authoritarian era. The chapter examines how two unexpected earthquakes in 2016, occurring only months after the opening of Asia’s first underground repository for low- and intermediate-level radioactive waste, brought renewed scrutiny to the contingent futures of nuclear energy. Though not causally related, this improbable convergence exposed tensions concerning technocratic risk management, democratic deliberation, and the long temporal horizons of radioactive waste stewardship. Situating these debates within the post-authoritarian politics of nuclear governance, the chapter argues that nuclear infrastructure poses ethical and political challenges that exceed the compressed timelines of technocratic governance and policy-making. In light of the multimillennial timescales of the nuclear fuel cycle, the chapter explores how ecological democracy might reframe risk and responsibility within larger questions of intergenerational accountability in the nuclear Anthropocene.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Artificial Intelligence at the crossroads: Productivity J-curve and carbon trade-offs in the global economy
articles.authors: Soumyajit Bhunia, Sneha Dey, Priya Mondal
articles.abstract: Artificial intelligence functions as an engine for increasing the productivity and economic growth of a nation. At the same time, it creates economic inequalities. The rapid development of the AI sector has profoundly transformed global systems, fundamentally altered operational paradigms, and paved novel pathways for economic growth. This study is trying to examine the intricate relationship between AI and economic growth, particularly on both the productivity gains and economic inequalities associated with AI adoption. We frame this scenario through the lens of productivity J-curve analysis. Using a panel of selected OECD countries spanning a period of eight years (2013-2020), the present study seeks to shed some light to examine the association among technological, political, and environmental factors, particularly AI patents and robot installation and their effects on productivity, measured by Total Factor Productivity (TFP). Furthermore, this study also focuses on the complex relationship between political factors, specifically democracy and political stability, and their impact on environmental degradation, measured by carbon emissions. To assess this relationship, we apply a novel econometric technique, the Seemingly Unrelated Regression Equation (SURE) estimation approach. Findings from our empirical study support the productivity J-curve hypothesis, where AI adoption at the early phase exhibits slower productivity growth, and the organizational changes catch up over time. It highlights the necessity for sustained organizational adaptation, realizing the full productivity gain of emerging technologies. Democratic systems frequently face delays in enacting and enforcing stringent environmental regulations due to political negotiations. Governments may prioritize economic development, often postponing environmental regulations to boost economic growth.

articles.title: Housing Commodification and Structural Dilemmas: An Ethnographic Study of "Atypical Street-Sleepers" in Hong Kong
articles.abstract: Hong Kong's status as a global financial hub, with its iconic skyline and capital concentration, belies a reality hidden in its urban interstices—under footbridges, in backstreets, and in all-night fast-food outlets—where a population of "street-sleepers" resides. These individuals are not homeless in the conventional sense of lacking shelter. Rather, they are often people who, under Hong Kong’s distinct socio-economic pressures, actively or passively adopt the streets as their "beds" and public spaces as their "homes." Drawing on fieldwork conducted between October 2024 and April 2025 in districts such as Prince Edward, Sham Shui Po, and Mong Kok East, this ethnographic study examines how institutional fragility in a global financial city reshapes life trajectories through housing commodification, leading to homelessness. Using a mixed-methods approach—including quantitative analysis of Sham Shui Po data, participatory ethnography, and life-history reconstruction of informants like "Auntie" and "Zhong Bo"—the research identifies a triple mechanism of risk transmission: policy discontinuity, family support disintegration, and employment polarization. The findings reveal that for many, street-sleeping is not an absolute "loss," but a passive "choice" amid systemic failure—a "shelter" for escaping trauma and preserving dignity. Ultimately, this paper argues that homelessness in Hong Kong stems from the convergence of its neoliberal governance, financialized housing regime, and residual welfare system. These life stories collectively depict how individuals are engulfed by systemic vulnerabilities, rendering this study not only a record of a marginalized group but also a critique of the Hong Kong development model and its human cost.

articles.title: Exploring attitudes and experiences of menopause in women from various cultures and ethnic groups: a scoping review
articles.authors: NIKKI JOANNE GARNER, Anna Sweeting, Jessica Marshall, Ishaani Thethy, Anne-Marie Minihane
articles.abstract: Background Ethnicity and culture influence menopause perceptions. Initiatives exist to address menopause disparities; however, inequalities persist, and despite policy efforts, women from various cultures and ethnic groups continue to feel excluded from mainstream menopause discourse. Objectives To understand women from various cultures and ethnic groups experiences of menopause and how attitudes towards menopause vary across ethnicities and cultures, to inform the development of more inclusive and culturally meaningful menopause research and care for all women. Eligibility Criteria Study focus was reported adult women’s experiences, attitudes, beliefs of menopause or discussions surrounding menopause. The study needed to be original research, report on a cultural/ethnic group, and reported in English. Sources of Evidence Database searches were conducted in November 2024, in PubMed, MEDLINE and on the first 25-pages of Google Scholar. Reference list searching was also conducted on excluded review studies. Charting Methods Studies were reviewed independently by two reviewers (NG/IT). Following selection, the main reviewer (NG) extracted study characteristics and participant demographics. Outcome data extraction was guided by the themes of language use, support perceptions, ethnic-group differences, and menopausal experiences or beliefs. Data was extracted independently by the main reviewer (NG), and a 10% random sample was checked by the second reviewer (JM), with full consensus reached after discussion. Results 2047 papers met initial criteria for inclusion. Data was extracted from 65 studies involving 67,434 women. Six themes emerged from the data: Cultural differences, Menopause as a natural process, Cessation of menses as a positive attribute of menopause, ‘Bad blood’ as a negative attribute of menopause, Role of spouse and Engagement in peer support. Conclusions Few studies offer cross-cultural analysis making comparisons between ethnicities and cultures difficult. Women from various cultures and ethnic groups attitudes and beliefs about menopause vary widely. Health professionals should ensure support aligns with women’s beliefs and preferences. Future research should explore how acculturation influences menopausal experiences among women from various cultures and ethnic groups.

articles.title: Politicians underestimate support for climate action even more than the general public
articles.authors: Timur Sevincer, Luisa Hostlowsky, Fenja Styhler, Wilhelm Hofmann
articles.abstract: Combatting climate change requires political action. However, whether politicians in democratic countries implement climate actions depends on their perception of the voters’ willingness to accept such actions. We surveyed a unique sample of 1,599 incumbent politicians from Germany and compared their perceptions of the publics’ willingness to support climate action with the actual willingness reported in two nationally representative samples of about 1,000 Germans each. Across political parties, politicians underestimated public acceptance of taxes and laws, and the willingness to contribute 1% of income to mitigate climate change. This pluralistic ignorance effect among politicians was even more pronounced than the pluralistic ignorance effect obtained in the population.

articles.title: Don’t Look Back in Anger: Humor, Self-Ironic Reflection, and the Structural Transformation of Retrospective Rage
articles.abstract: This paper theorizes how memories of harm and interpersonal conflict are transformed when the past is repeatedly recalled through anger, and how alternative pathways through humor, Self-Ironic Reflection, and structural understanding may become possible. Anger is an important emotion for detecting injustice, humiliation, and violations of dignity. However, when the past is viewed through anger alone, the target person may become cognitively magnified: no longer experienced as an ordinary human being who acted within a specific situation, but as a continuing threat that occupies the present psychological space. This paper conceptualizes this process as Retrospective Rage and Anger-Based Magnification. In response, the paper proposes Humor-Based Deflation as an alternative cognitive process. Humor, in this framework, does not mean trivializing harm or excusing the harmful actor. Rather, it refers to a practice through which the anger-magnified target is returned to human scale and the event becomes available for structural understanding. The paper further distinguishes between two pathways of inferiority and shame processing: one that converts inferiority into anger, domination, silence, and exclusion, and another that converts it into self-relativization, humor, learning, and structural reflection. In doing so, it connects anger memory to workplace harassment, organizational silence, and psychological safety. The central claim is that “Don’t Look Back in Anger” does not mean forgetting the past, suppressing anger, or excusing harm. Rather, it means refusing to let anger become the only form of retrospective understanding. By transforming memories of anger into humor, Self-Ironic Reflection, and structural analysis, personal pain can become public knowledge. The paper offers a theoretical framework for reconsidering workplace conflict, silence, harassment, and psychological safety through the paired processes of anger-based magnification and humor-based deflation.

articles.title: Salted Manananggal: A Cultural-Psychological Review of Behavioral Entrapment, Mixed Signals, and Emotional Limbo in I'm Drunk, I Love You (2017)
articles.abstract: This conceptual paper investigates the structural and psychological anatomy of romantic limbo by analyzing contemporary Philippine cinema through a localized, cultural-psychological lens. Specifically, this study utilizes the narrative framework of JP Habac's indie film I'm Drunk, I Love You (2017) to explore the subtle mechanisms that sustain unrequited love among contemporary Filipino youth. By stripping away localized horror tropes, the paper posits the mythological manananggal as a profound cultural allegory for unrequited love, representing permanent emotional bisection and physical fragmentation. It argues that a lover's suspension in this emotional limbo is driven by a powerful behavioral duality: intermittent reinforcement, where ambiguous mixed signals function as an addictive conditioning mechanism, and behavioral entrapment (the sunk-cost fallacy), where individuals remain trapped to justify their long-term psychological investments of youth, time, and effort. Finally, by deconstructing the local glorification of being marupok (soft-hearted), this paper explores how cultural spaces anchored in the core value of Kapwa (shared identity) provide a collective sanctuary for pining, reframing a one-sided existence as a deeply human, socially validated response to the agony of a love that remains perpetually almost within reach.

subjects.name: South and Southeast Asian Languages and Societies

articles.title: The Changing Educational Gradient in Non-Traditional Attitudes Toward Family Behaviour: A Cross-National Study
articles.authors: INVEST Flagship, Katrin Schwanitz, Lydia Palumbo, Ann Berrington, Marika Jalovaara
articles.abstract: Second Demographic Transition (SDT) theory highlights how secularisation and individualism dis-rupted traditional family behaviours, e.g., high fertility and lifelong universal marriage, in Western countries. While non-traditional family behaviours appeared in Nordic countries first, later diffus-ing to other regions, variations in approval across educational groups and historical time remain underexplored. This study examines approval levels towards non-traditional behaviours—voluntary childlessness, nonmarital cohabitation, nonmarital childbearing, parental divorce with children under 12, and mothers working with children under 3—across sociopolitical regimes in Europe, education levels, and historical time. European Social Survey data (2006, 2018) from 21 countries revealed different approval depending on the family behaviour. Approval was widespread for non-marital cohabitation, nonmarital childbearing, and mothers working, but voluntary childlessness and parental divorce were less accepted. Country differences were not always as predicted by SDT theory: Nordic countries showed the highest approval for all non-traditional family behaviours, followed by Southern Europe, while Western and Eastern Europe were more resistant. Approval of maternal employment and parental divorce varied by education, with higher approval among the highly educated. Regarding historical changes, in Southern Europe the initially higher approval among the highly educated for unmarried cohabitation and non-marital childbearing in 2006 had levelled out by 2018.

articles.title: Project EMPOWER: A Randomized Controlled Trial of Web-Based Single Session Intervention for Child Anxiety Among Financially Insecure Parents
articles.abstract: Background: Despite advancements in the treatment and implementation of evidence-based child anxiety interventions, up to 80% of anxious youth, especially those experiencing financial insecurity, never access care. Single session interventions (SSIs) offer a promising path toward increasing accessible, rapidly scalable interventions, given their clinical utility, potential for disseminability, and low cost. However, existing trials of child anxiety interventions have historically recruited socioeconomically homogeneous families (e.g. well-educated, affluent)—leaving their applicability to this high need and underserved population largely unknown. There is an urgent need to understand whether anxiety interventions designed for accessibility are effective for and acceptable to financially insecure families. Thus, this study evaluated the effects of Project EMPOWER, an evidence-based SSI designed to help reduce parental accommodation —a parenting behavior identified as a strong risk factor for anxiety in offspring — in financially insecure families. Methods: 101 parents endorsing financial insecurity with an anxious child aged 4-10 were recruited via CloudResearch. Parents self-reported on outcome measures and were randomized to an online, self-guided Project EMPOWER, or an informational control. All methods, hypothesis, and analytical plans were pre-registered on clincialtrials.gov (NCT05990322). Results. Relative to the control condition (N = 50), parents who received Project EMPOWER (N = 51) reported significant reductions in child’s anxiety (ds = 0.60, p = .02), and significant decreases in accommodation (ds= 0.87, p = .02) at 4-week follow-up. Parents who completed Project EMPOWER rated it as highly acceptable (M = 4.57 out of 5, SD = 0.47) and feasible (96.08% were full completers). Conclusions. Results support the effectiveness and feasibility of an online, self-guided SSIs for economically insecure families they are designed to help.

articles.title: It’s the Phone, Not Just the Person: Device-Driven Attrition in Smartphone-Tracking Research
articles.authors: Zoltan Kmetty, Bence Sagvari, Júlia Koltai
articles.abstract: Smartphone-tracking studies promise fine-grained behavioral data, but participants must pass through a demanding sequence of stages, and cumulative attrition can bias the analytic sample. Most existing evidence rests on stated willingness rather than realized behavior and treats the device as a simple iOS-versus-Android coverage problem. Using a Hungarian smartphone-tracking study that combined a survey with passive data collection, we follow actual participation from consent through questionnaire completion, app installation, and sustained data transmission, a late stage invisible to willingness-based designs. Attrition at each stage is modeled with logistic regressions. Individual characteristics explain little of the drop-out, and, consistent with the broader gap between stated willingness and realized behavior, hard demographic characteristics weigh more on actual participation than soft attitudinal ones. The phone manufacturer is the single most informative predictor, with effects concentrated at installation and, most strongly, at data transmission, and largely independent of trust, privacy concern, proficiency, and personality. Newer operating-system versions are protective while price tier is not, pointing to vendor background-process policies rather than hardware quality or user affluence. The device should be treated as a first-class source of selection in smartphone-tracking research.

articles.title: Green Cities, Red Barns: The Rural-Urban Divide on Environmental Politics in the United States
articles.authors: Patrick Hunnicutt, Olivia Grace Jessee, B. Kal Munis
articles.abstract: Over the last thirty years, American politics has seen two key developments: intensified geographic polarization and the proliferation of environmental policy conflicts, most recently concerning climate change. While these developments are intuitively related, political science research has yet to investigate whether a rural-urban divide in environmental policy attitudes exists in the United States and, if so, why. Here we triangulate between two nationally representative surveys and an original survey conducted in Oregon to explore rural-urban differences in environmental policy attitudes. We find that rural residency is robustly associated with lower levels of support for various environmental policies, even after accounting for compositional differences between rural and urban communities (e.g., partisanship). Moreover, rural Oregonians express markedly lower levels of external efficacy regarding environmental policymaking and trust in environmental agencies and interest groups, highlighting how procedural concerns may compound partisan and distributive conflict in shaping rural opposition to environmental policy.

articles.title: Left Behind by Liberalization? Cultural Grievances across Regions in Britain and France, 1987-2017
articles.abstract: The radical right has found electoral success in rural and industrial, but less so in thriving urban areas. This spatially concentrated success has led to the assumption that regions have been drifting apart politically in recent decades, with residents of ‘left-behind places’ expressing growing cultural grievances (socially conservative attitudes). We investigate this assumption using post-election surveys from Britain and France spanning the 1980s-2010s. Analysis reveals widening gaps in cultural grievances between central cities and industrial regions. Contrary to popular belief, this polarization is not due to industrial regions increasing their cultural grievances over time, but to central city regions becoming more progressive. Thus, industrial regions remain as opposed to immigration and European integration today as in the 1980s, while London and much of France have become more progressive. These findings highlight the need for research to examine the drivers of attitudinal change in central city areas alongside economically declining regions.

articles.title: Left Behind? Regional Change in Household Income and Radical Right Voting in the United Kingdom, the United States, France, Italy, and Germany
articles.abstract: Over the past decades, the populist radical right (PRR) was particularly successful in rural, peripheral or former industrial regions. This unequal geography of support has fuelled the left-behind narrative, which posits that regional economic decline drives PRR support. Strongly articulated after Brexit, this narrative has gained traction in explaining populism’s rise across Europe and beyond. We test this narrative by analysing pivotal votes in five major Western democracies, where the PRR achieved breakthrough success: the 2016 Brexit referendum, the 2016 election of Trump, the 2017 French presidential election, the 2022 success of Meloni’s Fratelli d’Italia, and the 2025 German federal election. We distinguish electoral units at a detailed level of counties, provinces, and districts and assess how regional economic decline, measured by relative decline in household median income, correlates with PRR voting. We find large country differences. The left-behind narrative is consistent, albeit weakly, with the regional voting pattern for Brexit and for Le Pen in France. However, it does not work for Germany and the US: Support for the AfD in 2025 and Trump in 2016 was stronger in districts and counties that, although poorer initially, were catching up in terms of household income. In Italy, the narrative fails completely, with the PRR thriving in prosperous provinces that were further pulling ahead. These findings caution against extrapolating the left-behind narrative from Britain to Germany, Italy and the US.

articles.title: Considerations for Vehicle Residents Experiencing Homelessness: A Review of Western States Point-in-Time Counts
articles.authors: Sam Lutzker, Ruth Tretter, Linda Barringer, Graham Pruss
articles.abstract: Introduction: The U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development requires Continuums of Care (CoCs) to conduct point-in-time (PiT) counts of sheltered and unsheltered homelessness, but local reporting practices vary. This impacts the official recognition of and planning for people sleeping in vehicles, a substantial but inconsistently measured segment of unsheltered homelessness. Methods: In collaboration with the National Vehicle Residency Coalition, we reviewed publicly available 2024/2023 PiT count reports for 73 of 81 CoCs in the U.S. Census West Region. Results: Thirty-eight CoC reports (52.05%) disaggregated vehicular homelessness. Across these CoCs, an estimated 46,771 people slept in vehicles, representing 38.79% of the unsheltered and 23.84% of the homeless populations counted. Reported shares of unsheltered homelessness in vehicles ranged from 9.38% to 71.27%, reflecting both geographic variation and substantial methodological heterogeneity. Discussion: We identify methodological challenges for PiT counts, including inconsistent sampling and reporting practices that impede cross-CoC comparison and development of national-level estimates of vehicular homelessness. PiT counts should improve through standardization, including consistently sampling and disaggregating vehicular homelessness.

articles.title: An Open Framework for Delineating and Classifying Retail Centres across the United Kingdom
articles.authors: Owen Goodwin, Zihan Yin, Zi Ye, Alex Singleton, Les Dolega
articles.abstract: Retail centres, including town centres, high streets and retail parks, are critical to the economic vitality and social cohesion of communities across the United Kingdom. Yet spatial definitions of these centres remain inconsistent, hampering comparative analysis and evidence-based planning. This paper presents a new open-source methodology for delineating retail centre boundaries at national scale. The approach integrates Foursquare Places point-of-interest data with Open- StreetMap land-use polygons, applying a comprehensive quality-filtering procedure before two parallel detection pathways: H3 hexagonal tessellation with network-based connected- component identification for traditional walkable centres, and brand-matching heuristics for car-oriented retail parks. Two further innovations refine the output: land-use-informed removal of false positives in non-retail employment areas, and the application of Leiden community detection to partition large urban agglomerations into functionally distinct sub-centres. The methodology delineates 9,477 retail centres across the United Kingdom, compared with 6,423 in the previous national dataset, with substantially improved coverage in Scotland and Northern Ireland and explicit, reproducible identification of retail parks. Validation through stakeholder workshops involving academic researchers, local authority planners, and commercial sector representatives confirms that the resulting boundaries align with planning practice and local knowledge. A case study of Liverpool’s night-time economy demonstrates the framework’s practical utility, showing how a consistent spatial unit enables integration of heterogeneous sources, including event listings, live-music gig data, and late-opening commercial units, to characterise urban evening activity. Built on openly available data, the framework is reproducible and supports ongoing monitoring of UK retail landscapes

articles.title: “Safe for now?” Boundary work and tensions of AI Adoption in Sign Language Interpreting and Speech-to-Text Reporting in Belgium and the Netherlands
articles.authors: Maartje De Meulder, Karolien Gebruers, Deborah Giustini
articles.abstract: This article examines how sign language interpreters (SLIs) and speech-to-text reporters (STTRs) in Belgium (Flanders) and the Netherlands understand and navigate artificial intelligence (AI)-driven language technologies in their professional lives. Drawing on an online survey (N = 134) conducted in 2025, and theorized through boundary work, we identify three tensions structuring practitioners' accounts: a temporality tension, in which anticipated disruption coexists with present stability; an access tension, in which AI expands available options while enabling institutions to narrow the definition of "sufficient access"; and a professional identity tension, in which the technology threatening practitioners' livelihoods may simultaneously advance the profession's own emancipatory ideal. We argue that AI in language access is actively produced through practitioners’ ongoing boundary work and calls for recognizing them — alongside deaf consumers — as epistemic actors in governing just technological transitions.

articles.title: Statistical Learning Shapes Cognitive Maps of Signed Social Networks
articles.authors: Yi-Fei Hu, Marc-Lluís Vives, Apoorva Bhandari, Oriel FeldmanHall
articles.abstract: Building cognitive maps of signed social networks—those containing both friendly and antagonistic ties—is essential for navigating social life. These cognitive maps are often not faithful reproductions of social networks but instead exhibit systematic biases. While dominant accounts attribute these biases to schemas, such as ones derived from Balance Theory, they leave open the question of where such biases come from in the first place. We propose that these biases emerge from statistical learning of real-world relational patterns. Across four studies that combine behavioral experiments, computational modeling, and analyses of ten longitudinal signed networks (over 150,000 nodes and 1.6 million ties), we find that people’s biases simply reflect statistical structures of their social environments. Biases align more closely with naturally occurring motifs than the patterns predicted by Balance Theory. When exposed to artificial networks that violate real-world statistics, individuals gradually, yet flexibly alter their biases, implicating statistical learning as the underlying mechanism. These findings position statistical learning as a foundational process shaping how humans encode and reason about complex signed social networks, providing a new lens for understanding socio-cognitive map building.

articles.title: Do voters want (and reward) stricter political finance regulation? Evidence from South Africa.
articles.abstract: Efforts to reduce political corruption often focus on stricter regulation of, and enhanced transparency about, political finance. Do voters favor such reforms, and do they reward (and punish) parties that enact (retrench) them? We examine these questions in South Africa, where the ruling African National Congress (ANC) introduced — and later effectively reversed— the Political Party Funding Act (PPFA), which de-anonymized donors and capped contributions. Using a nationally representative survey (N = 1800) with embedded experiments, we measure voter support for such regulations and test how information about the reform and its reversal shapes voter preferences. Although respondents generally express support for tighter regulations, they do not become more favorable toward the ANC after learning about the PPFA. Information about the reversal decreases declared ANC vote intention by around 5 percentage points. Auxiliary tests suggest that voters' doubts about the enforceability of the Act may explain the limited positive updating.

articles.title: Insta-Famous or Invisible: Social Media Use and Belonging at a Public Flagship University
articles.abstract: Social media and U.S. public universities hold as-yet-unrealized potential for equity. Both institutions, however, also serve as reminders of social inequality while normalizing relatively privileged lifestyles. Past scholarship highlights how symbolic capital, felt stigma, and self-inflicted symbolic violence can maintain inequality, yet their operation across interconnected digital and higher educational contexts remains under-analyzed. Through 51 interviews with students at a U.S. public flagship university, I investigate how social media use impacts perceived inequality in higher education. Social media—particularly image-centric platforms prompting users to share visual representations of their offline lives—more readily aided the accumulation of symbolic capital and digital prestige among students better equipped to emulate culturally dominant lifestyle content through proximity to hegemonic norms. The same platforms more readily amplified exposure to felt stigma and experiences with digital non-recognition among students distanced from these norms. Students experiencing felt stigma could exhibit learned invalidation, considering their lives unworthy of certain forms of digital documentation. Further, women students faced greater pressure to document their lives through image-centric platforms. I highlight a cross-institutional context where digital comparisons intertwine with access to perceived-vital resources for social integration and upward mobility, revealing digital platforms’ potential to intensify unequal experiences in higher education.

articles.title: Wearing Many Hats: How Rural U.S. Newspaper Journalists Negotiate Professional Identity Amid Digital and Economic Disruption
articles.authors: Sayyed Fawad Ali Shah, Teddi A. Joyce, Breann E. Murphy, Ben Cunningham, Gregory P Perreault
articles.abstract: Our study seeks to interrogate how local journalists conceptualize professionalism and negotiate their professional role-identities amid economic and technological disruption. Based on 17 in-depth interviews, we found that journalists idealized journalistic values such as objectivity, accuracy, and community service. However, job insecurity, lack of resources, increased workload, inability of their organizations to keep up with the changing nature of journalism in the emerging digital media landscape, and juggling different responsibilities in their organizations, shaped their professional experiences and routines. Our participants also noted that the traditional separation between editorial and marketing departments in their organizations had evaporated or shrunk, although most viewed collaboration between these departments as necessary for organizational survival rather than a threat to journalistic autonomy. The findings highlight how organizational culture and economic instability are reshaping professional identities and journalistic practices in rural news environments. Based on the findings of our study, we suggest ways in which news organizations, journalism schools, and local press associations could help local newspapers.

articles.title: How Much War Builds a State? Fiscal Bargaining in the Shadow of War
articles.authors: Daniel A. N. Goldstein, Yusuf Magiya
articles.abstract: We present a formal model that examines how external conflict reshapes the bargaining process over fiscal control between a central state and a peripheral elite. Our findings reveal an inverted U-shaped relationship between war-related pressures and state fiscal control. Low-to-moderate war pressure enhances state fiscal control by strengthening the state's bargaining power. However, excessive pressure undermines state control. This outcome is influenced by the degree to which the interests of the state and the elites align. We also identify instances when states fall into a `capacity trap,' where those with low initial fiscal control sacrifice future revenues to finance ongoing conflict. Using historical fiscal revenue data from Europe and case studies of Ancien Régime France and the Ottoman Empire, we test the predictions and find supporting evidence. These findings refine bellicist theory by highlighting the contingent benefits of conflict on state development when accounting for the complexities involved in state financing.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Dynamic Climate Risk and the Vulnerability Ratchet
articles.authors: Michael Weisberg, Koko Warner, Zinta Zommers, Youssef Nassef, Simon Levin, Erol Akcay
articles.abstract: Changing climate poses increasing risk to communities worldwide. Climate risk is a complex phenomenon that emerges from the interaction of physical, biological, and social systems. The dominant framework for assessing climate risk, developed across successive IPCC assessments, focuses on hazard, vulnerability, and exposure as contributing components of climate risk. This framework has organized research and policy productively, but it remains limited as it represents these components separate inputs evaluated at a single point in time. As a snapshot, it cannot capture path dependence, compounding, or irreversibility, the properties that make climate risk most difficult to address. To address this limitation, we introduce a Dynamic Climate Risk (DCR) framework that makes the coupling between risk components explicit and tracks their co-evolution over time. Analyzing the hazard–vulnerability coupling, we show that when hazard events outpace recovery, vulnerability fails to return to baseline and instead drifts upward in a self-reinforcing “vulnerability ratchet.” We derive the threshold event frequency at which the ratchet engages, and a lock-in vulnerability beyond which recovery capacity collapses, defining a finite policy window whose length we bound analytically. Mitigation remains essential over the long run, but because hazard is effectively fixed on near-term policy horizons while vulnerability and exposure are not, the most consequential near-term choices concern managing these couplings rather than emissions alone. We illustrate the framework with three cases, Bangladesh, the 2022 Pakistan floods, and the divergent trajectories of Haiti and Cuba, each exercising different parts of its structure.

articles.title: Data Collected, Intelligence Lost: The Structural Suppression of Learning in Community-Based MEAL Systems in Post-Conflict Northern Uganda
articles.abstract: Monitoring, Evaluation, Accountability and Learning (MEAL) systems across community-based programs in Sub-Saharan Africa are predominantly architected to satisfy upward donor reporting obligations rather than to support adaptive program management. This paper draws on field observations from child protection, education, livelihood, and youth programming across a post-conflict district to diagnose a structural misalignment between what MEAL systems measure and what field-level programs actually need to learn. Using the principal-agent framework, the analysis shows that local implementing organizations, as agents, are compelled to optimize data flows for the informational needs of distant donors, as principals, at the systematic expense of internal and downward learning. Five practitioner arenas are examined: school dropout reintegration, caregiver engagement, community feedback mechanisms, Village Savings and Loan Associations (VSLAs), and youth-led monitoring. Each is analyzed through a consistent diagnostic lens: the compliance metric extracted for upward reporting, the operational intelligence suppressed by aggregation, and the low-cost adaptive pivot achievable without additional data collection burden. Situating this diagnosis within complex adaptive systems theory and the Made in Africa Evaluation paradigm, the paper argues that post-conflict, rural contexts operate as non-linear systems in which rigid logframe architectures systematically produce epistemological blind spots. The core finding is not that organizations collect insufficient data; it is that data already sitting in case files, VSLA ledgers, scorecard registers, and youth monitoring logs is seldom repurposed for learning. The paper concludes with a zero-added-burden operational blueprint for practicing evaluators, offering concrete low-cost internal review mechanisms that can be embedded within existing MEAL infrastructure.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: “We are alive and dead at the same time”: A Children’s Rights Normative Culture Analysis of Children’s Rights in Gaza
articles.authors: Marieke Hopman, Ayed Abu Eqtaish, Kristen Cheney, Guleid Ahmed Jama
articles.abstract: This paper aims to understand the current children’s rights situation in Gaza, to analyse what current factors create and sustain this situation, and to present possible pathways for positive change. We apply the Children’s Rights Normative Culture (CRNC) model (Hopman et al. 2026) to a dataset including 88 research conversations in Gaza (76 children, 12 adults) and qualitative analysis of over 4000 textual sources. The findings reveal the collapse of almost all conditions necessary for the realisation of children’s rights, including a lack of access to essential resources, the breakdown of societal structures in Gaza, and the dehumanisation of Gazan children in the Israeli CRNC. Together, these conditions have turned Gaza into a “torturing environment” for children. Lastly, the paper suggests pathways for positive change. These include the restoration of Gaza’s civilian infrastructure, requiring an end to all violence; child participation in decision-making around reconstruction; and awareness-raising in Israel.

articles.title: Do Economics Students Under- or Overestimate Income Inequality
articles.abstract: This paper reports on a novel variation on an interactive classroom exercise aimed at getting students to think about and estimate levels of income inequality in the United Kingdom and globally. Existing inequality elicitation procedures usually require participants to put amounts or percentages to different points in the distribution. The current exercise reverses the direction and asks participants to estimate where various salary amounts are located in the income distribution. This approach is inspired by the observation that in the real world, when thinking about the income distribution, we often start with a salary amount, and we compare this with the rest of the income distribution. Contrary to the majority of the previous literature, which finds systematic underestimation of inequality, results (n=30) indicate that students overestimate inequality. For most income levels, respondents placed salary amounts lower in the distribution than they actually sit, implying a belief that high incomes are more prevalent than they are. The overestimation is especially pronounced in the global context. The paper further proposes a method for transforming the student's guesses into approximate Lorenz curves to facilitate visual comparison with actual distributions.

articles.title: Doubleweave Phenomenology: A Cross-Indigenous Method for Weaving Aniyvwiya (Cherokee) and Kānaka Maoli (Native Hawaiian) Lived Experience
articles.abstract: Indigenous and decolonizing inquiry has produced influential metaphors for bringing knowledges together — braiding and Two-Eyed Seeing chief among them — yet both join Indigenous knowledge with Western knowledge and tend toward a single blended result. This article develops doubleweave phenomenology, a cross-Indigenous method that weaves the lived experience of two Indigenous peoples into one analytic structure while keeping each whole and distinct. Grounded in Cherokee doubleweave basketry, in which a single weaver forms two complete interlocking walls in one basket, the method extends Moustakas’s modified Stevick– Colaizzi–Keen phenomenology by treating the researcher as the first co-researcher and weaving her people’s account beside a second people’s on a loom of story. I present the model’s epistemological, ontological, and axiological commitments; its components; its distinctions from related approaches; and a brief Aniyvwiya (Cherokee) and Kānaka Maoli (Native Hawaiian) demonstration. Reciprocity is theorized as a condition of rigor rather than a threat to it, and the method is situated within Indigenous data-sovereignty ethics. Keywords: Indigenous methodologies; phenomenology; doubleweave; cross-Indigenous inquiry; talk story (kūkākūkā); survivance; decolonizing research; Kānaka Maoli (Native Hawaiian); Aniyvwiya (Cherokee)

articles.title: Oregon Traffic Safety Research Roadmap
articles.authors: Greg Phillip Griffin, Josh F. Roll
articles.abstract: To address the rising increase in fatal and serious traffic injuries, the ODOT Traffic Safety Research Roadmap establishes a structured, five-year research agenda listing research concepts for funding through ODOT Research Unit’s annual funding cycle as well as other funding opportunities. The study identifies 51 priority traffic safety research needs, spanning the Safe Systems topics of safe people, safe vehicles, safe speeds, safe roads, and post-crash care. An implementation playbook suggests pathways of achieving the desired research needs to support transportation safety practice.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Restoring Social Functioning among Women Survivors of Repeated Violence through Trauma-Informed Care-Based Casework Interventions
articles.abstract: Violence against women is a social problem that has serious impacts on victims’ mental health, physical well-being, and social functioning. This study aims to describe the process of restoring social functioning in a woman survivor of repeated violence through a Trauma-Informed Care (TIC)-based casework intervention at UPTD PPS Griya Wanita Mandiri, West Java Province. The study employed a qualitative approach with a single case study design, enabling an in-depth, holistic, and contextual exploration of one case. The case involved an adult woman who experienced repeated violence throughout her life course, beginning in childhood and continuing into adulthood through five marriages, all of which were characterized by domestic violence. These experiences resulted in complex trauma affecting her physical, psychological, social, and spiritual dimensions of functioning. Data were collected through in-depth interviews, direct observation, and document review. Data were analyzed using the interactive model of Miles and Huberman. The rehabilitation process was conducted over a three-month period and involved biopsychosocial-spiritual assessment, individual counseling, psychological referral, social guidance, spiritual and mental guidance, habit formation activities, vocational skills training, and family reintegration support. The findings indicate that Trauma-Informed Care-based casework contributed to improvements in the client’s social functioning. Positive changes were reflected in reduced anxiety, decreased frequency of seizures, increased self-confidence, improved social interaction, and renewed communication with family members. Family reintegration emerged as an important factor that strengthened the recovery process by providing emotional support and enhancing the sustainability of positive changes achieved during rehabilitation. This study highlights the importance of integrating Trauma-Informed Care principles into social rehabilitation services for women survivors of repeated violence and underscores the strategic role of social workers in facilitating comprehensive recovery processes. Keywords: social functioning; violence against women; Trauma-Informed Care; casework; social rehabilitation

articles.title: Physiological Encoding of Social Evaluation: Modeling Eye Movements, Pupillary, and Neuronal Responses to Faces
articles.authors: Mao Anqi, Runnan Cao, Sai Sun, Shuo Wang, Daniel DongWon Oh
articles.abstract: Face-based social evaluations are ubiquitous and consequential. These evaluations are organized along two fundamental dimensions: valence (positive versus negative intentions) and power (high versus low social influence). However, how these dimensions manifest in spontaneous physiological responses remains unclear. Across three studies (96 participants and 506 single neurons), we used data-driven modeling to identify dimension-specific physiological signatures. Positive evaluations along the valence dimension were encoded by pupillary constriction and decreased neural activity in both amygdala and hippocampus. Amygdalar suppression was feature-specific, emerging only during mouth fixations, whereas the hippocampus exhibited feature-invariant suppression across all fixations. Power evaluations were encoded by pupil dilation, prolonged eye-region fixations, and increased hippocampal activity. Furthermore, social anxiety selectively heightened the sensitivity of pupillary responses to valence but did not affect power encoding. This selective modulation confirms that these responses are driven by high-level social computations rather than reflexive reactions to low-level visual features. Our findings demonstrate how the fundamental dimensions of social evaluation are embodied across multiple physiological systems within moments of viewing a face.

articles.title: The Relationship Between Stereotypes About Nationality and Stereotypes About Race/Ethnicity
articles.abstract: Nationality groups such as Mexican and Japanese Americans have long been subjected to distinct forms of stereotyping and discrimination in the U.S. Concurrently, nationality groups have been subsumed into broader racial/ethnic identities and stereotyped based on associated racial/ethnic groups. Across four studies, we examined when nationality groups’ stereotypes diverge from or converge with racial/ethnic stereotypes (i.e., stereotype distance). In Study 1 (N = 7,050), we measured 11 stereotypes of 28 nationality groups and 6 racial/ethnic groups. Generally, Mexican Americans were stereotyped similarly to Hispanic or Latino Americans and East Asian nationality groups were stereotyped similarly to Asian Americans. White and Black Americans were stereotyped differently from associated nationality groups. In Study 2 (N = 630), we measured two constructs as explanations for stereotype distance: nationality groups’ racial/ethnic prototypicality and racial/ethnic groups’ perceived likelihood of identification with race/ethnicity over nationality. In Study 3, we demonstrated that racial/ethnic prototypicality and identification with race/ethnicity over nationality were related to stereotype distance. In Study 4, Census data revealed relationships between stereotype distance and real-world immigration patterns. In a nation of immigrants, these findings demonstrate how nationality and race/ethnicity jointly—and sometimes separately—structure stereotypes.

articles.title: Family Context, Directed and Random Exploration Profiles, and Child Socioemotional Functioning
articles.authors: Zhi Li, Yuanzhe Li, Patrick Davies, Melissa Sturge-Apple
articles.abstract: Guided by the evolutionary developmental perspective, this multi-method, longitudinal study seeks to examine “hyperparameters” that organize young children’s learning. In particular, we adopt computational modeling and a person-centered approach to characterize children’s exploration-exploitation profiles during a reward-probability task. We also explored the family socioeconomic precursors and child psychological sequelae of the profiles. Participants were 243 families with a preschooler (Mean age=4.60 years, 56% girls) from diverse racial and ethnic backgrounds (46% Black, 19% Latinx). Family socioeconomic risks were measured at wave one, and exploration-exploitation and socioemotional functioning were assessed two years later via a reward probability task and teacher-report, respectively. Person-centered approach revealed three exploration-exploitation profiles, reflecting (a) a high-random exploration, (b) a high-directed exploration, and (c) a balanced exploration-exploitation profile. Greater family socioeconomic risk was linked to an elevated likelihood of membership in the high-random-exploration profile (e.g., Z = -2.37). In turn, the high-random-exploration profile exhibited the highest attention problems and greater externalizing problems. Findings have critical implications for understanding the processes through which children adapt to adverse family contexts.

articles.title: Boomers vs. Zoomers? Age Stereotypes and Fairness Evaluations in the Workplace
articles.authors: Marcel Knobloch, Sophia Hunger, Lena Hipp
articles.abstract: According to public media debates, today's workplaces are characterized by generational tensions, citing stereotypes such as "lazy Gen Z" and "irresponsible Boomers.” Whether these perceived generational tensions manifest in age-based stereotypes and differential evaluations in workplace settings, however, remains an open question. To address this question, we draw on unique survey data from Germany (N = 2,229) to provide a nuanced exploration of cohort-specific stereotypes and their impact on fairness evaluations of workplace behaviors. Building on theories of status characteristics and social identity, we examine how age shapes perceptions, stereotypes, attitudes, and discriminatory behaviors. First, we conducted a WikiSurvey to explore common stereotypes about younger and older individuals, revealing the positive and negative perceptions respondents hold toward both their own and other age groups. Second, we replicate survey questions from the European Social Survey on ageism with the same respondents. We find that while younger people view both their peers and older individuals positively, older people tend to favor their own age group. Third, we conducted a survey experiment to assess the causal effect of age on evaluations of workplace behaviors and find that older respondents evaluate the behaviors of potential older co-workers as fairer than the exact same behaviors of younger co-workers. There is, however, no statistically significant difference between younger respondents’ views on the same behavior for older and younger col-leagues. Taken together, our findings provide evidence for age-based in-group favoritism, in particular among older people, which may have significant consequences for collaboration, productivity, and organizational cohesion.

articles.title: Evaluating Nonuniform Dependability Across Response Conditions: A Conditional Generalizability Framework Illustrated in Automated Essay Scoring
articles.abstract: Aggregate reliability and dependability estimates can obscure heterogeneity in measurement-design burden across response conditions; when this occurs, a single G- or D-study may mischaracterize the adequacy of a design for particular strata. This study introduces a conditional generalizability framework with three components. First, automated scoring configurations—the encoder architectures and scoring-head families admissible within a fixed development pipeline—are conceptualized as a universe of admissible measurement conditions rather than as incidental modeling choices. Second, analytical D-study projections are compared with empirical configuration sweeps over a finite scoring pool, yielding two estimands of design adequacy whose agreement or divergence diagnoses the realized configuration universe. Third, G- and D-study evidence is conditioned on entropy-defined response strata, treating entropy as an operational stratification variable, not as a construct claim about writing quality, so that both the aggregate and condition-specific design burden can be evaluated. Whereas recent extensions of generalizability theory have addressed AI-generated item variants on the response side, the present framework addresses an analogous scoring-side problem: AI-mediated scoring configurations. The framework is demonstrated using automated essay scoring of timed L2 writing tasks. The realized scoring design was dependable in aggregate (Φ ≈ .76 for the person × task × configuration design). Re-estimated at the essay level within entropy strata, dependability was uniformly high but declined modestly and robustly across strata (Φ = .88, .87, .84)—a small gradient that nonetheless implied different decision-study requirements, with the highest-entropy stratum needing more crossed scoring conditions to reach a given dependability target. Empirical configuration sweeps further indicate whether analytical minimum-design recommendations are attainable within the available scoring pool. The framework provides a portable workflow for evaluating nonuniform dependability whenever multiple scoring, rating, or observation configurations are available.

articles.title: From Plan to Pram: Type of Partnership in the Formation and Realisation of Fertility Intentions in Czechia
articles.abstract: Partnership forms in Europe have diversified: cohabitation encompasses unions that range from steps towards, to long-term alternatives to, marriage. Yet it remains unclear whether these distinctions shape solely the formation of short-term fertility intentions or also their realisation as births. Research into the significance of cohabitation has examined how intentions form without considering their translation into behaviour, whereas research into the intention–realisation gap has addressed cohabitation as a single, undifferentiated category. Employing data from two panel rounds of the Czech Generations and Gender Survey, each comprising a baseline and three-year follow-up (round 1: 2005 and 2008; round 2: 2020–2022 and 2025), binary and multinomial logistic regression was applied to examine whether the importance of partnership type differed at these two stages for women and men aged 18–44 in coresidential partnerships. The study distinguished married individuals, cohabitants who intended to marry and those without such intentions. Partnership type strongly differentiates the formation of fertility intentions: cohabitants who intend to marry are most likely to plan a child, whereas those without marriage intentions are substantially less likely to do so. These differences appeared to narrow over time (albeit marginally), in line with the normalisation of cohabitation. In contrast, no evidence was found that the legal or intentional character of the union is independently associated with realisation, once parity and intention certainty were accounted for. Overall, partnership type structures selection into fertility intentions, whereas their realisation appears to depend more on life-course position and motivational commitment than on institutional form.

articles.title: Language Development: Input, Multilingualism, and the Literacy Environment - A Blue Blocks Montessori School Home Observation Study, Hyderabad
articles.authors: Pavan Goyal, Sreemoyee Chakraborty, Raunak Sharma
articles.abstract: The present study investigates the home language environment of 99 children from Blue Blocks Montessori School, Hyderabad, for the period January-February 2026, focusing on three interrelated areas of interest: (i) amount and quality of adults’ linguistic input, (ii) type of the multilingual environment, and (iii) nature of home literacy environment. Based on the Montessori principle of the sensitive period for language (Montessori, 1949; Montessori, 1946) and recent research concerning language learning, bilingualism and early literacy (Hart & Risley, 1995; Cummins, 2000; Neuman & Dickinson, 2001), we analysed parental reports on story time and song singing, narrations of activities, conversation richness, and the variety and distribution of sources of input and information within a multilingual context, as well as reading practices and presence of books. Key findings include: a majority of children receive more than one hour of extended adult narrative daily; 88% live in multilingual households; mother is the primary language-input source in 43% of families; structured one-person-one-language practice is rarely maintained; mealtime conversation is predominantly child-led; and home literacy environments are moderately rich but with significant variability in book quantity and dedicated reading infrastructure. Parent reflective data illuminate a community in active, joyful language development. Implications for Montessori language pedagogy and home-school language partnership are discussed.

articles.title: Electoral systems shape the entry and entrenchment of extremist parties
articles.authors: Chanuwas Aswamenakul, Tyler Marghetis, Brad LeVeck, Paul E. Smaldino
articles.abstract: Representative democracies aspire to represent voters’ viewpoints through their elected officials. At the same time, they may try to minimize extremist viewpoints that can destabilize democracy itself. Here we show that electoral systems navigate this tension by trading off between two vulnerabilities: extremist entry and extremist entrenchment. Using computational models, we investigate how electoral systems respond to the emergence of ideological extremism arising either through the introduction of a new party or through the gradual radicalization of an established party. Systems that more accurately represent public opinion are more permissive to new parties, including extremist ones. Conversely, systems that suppress extremist entry can also protect incumbent parties as they drift toward increasingly extreme and unrepresentative positions. Electoral systems therefore differ not only in how well they represent voters or how many parties they sustain, but in the vulnerabilities they create: institutional openness increases the risk of extremist entry, while institutional inertia increases the risk of extremist entrenchment. Democracies thus face a fundamental design challenge: The same institutional features that protect a democracy from one extremist risk also expose it to another.

articles.title: How Americans Spend their Time Online
articles.authors: David Lazer, Burak Ozturan, Christo Wilson, David Choffnes, Cassidy Waldrip, Hsiu Chi,Lu, John Wihbey
articles.abstract: We examine where people spend their time online, analyzing desktop browsing data collected from the National Internet Observatory (NIO). Key findings include: ● A few platforms dominate where we spend our time. The top 10 platforms captured about half (49%) of all online time, with Gmail at number one with 16.4%. ● A tiny number of companies dominate where we spend time. Most user attention is funneled through a handful of corporations. Alphabet (Google’s parent company) alone accounts for 35% of total browsing time in our sample, followed by Meta (8.9%), Microsoft (5.1%), Yahoo (4.3%), and Amazon (3.0%). These five companies capture more than half of all time Americans spend online on desktop. ● Americans spend half their online time on just two things: social media (25.3%) and email (24.9%). ● AI is already bigger than news. Large Language Model (LLM) tools, such as ChatGPT, now account for 2.9% of total online time, while news is only 2.5%. ● The Web is highly age-segregated. Users over 65 spend 36.7% of their online time on email and 14% on Facebook, versus 11% and 2%, respectively, for users under 30. Older people are still using Yahoo and AOL for email (9%, 2%, respectively), both of which barely register among younger users. The oldest cohort spends 4.7% of their time on news sites, far more than the youngest cohort (1.2%). In contrast, users under 30 spend 13% of their time on YouTube, 9% on Google apps (Docs, Drive, Meet, Classroom, and other Google services excluding Gmail, Search, and YouTube), and 3% on ChatGPT, as compared to 6%, 3%, and <1%, respectively, among the oldest cohort.

articles.title: Youth Unemployment and Mismatch in the Turkish Labour Market
articles.abstract: This paper analyses youth unemployment and education–job mismatch in Turkey using individual-level data from the Turkish Statistical Institute(TÜİK) Labour Force Statistics for 2015.
